target_output.data_model: report_publication (slug TEXT PK, title TEXT, year INT, country_region TEXT[] [INDEX], thematic_area TEXT[] [INDEX], authors TEXT[], abstract TEXT, pdf_asset_path TEXT, pdf_size_mb REAL, data_appendix_path TEXT, published_date DATE, featured BOOL). author_profile (slug TEXT PK, name TEXT, title TEXT, bio TEXT, photo_path TEXT). press_release (slug TEXT PK, title TEXT, published_date DATE, summary TEXT, body_markdown TEXT, pdf_path TEXT). thematic_area (slug TEXT PK, label TEXT, description TEXT). All content stored as YAML frontmatter in Markdown files, ingested by Eleventy collections API at build time into in-memory JavaScript objects for template rendering and Pagefind indexing.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Client interactions: Pagefind JavaScript client performs search against pre-built static index shards (GET /pagefind/pagefind.js, GET /pagefind/index/*.pf_index). PDF downloads served directly from Netlify CDN edge (GET /assets/reports/{slug}.pdf). Matomo analytics beacon (POST https://analytics.ngo-internal.org/matomo.php) for page views and PDF download events. No authentication endpoints. No form submission endpoints -- contact forms handled via Netlify Forms (no third-party JS required).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Netlify _headers file enforces on all paths: Content-Security-Policy (default-src 'self'; style-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; object-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'none'; base-uri 'self'); Strict-Transport-Security (max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload); X-Frame-Options DENY; X-Content-Type-Options nosniff; Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin; Permissions-Policy (camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()). Tailwind CSS built locally via PostCSS -- no external CDN style-src required. Subresource integrity applied to any future third-party script. Branch protection on main requires at least one reviewer approval before merge. GitHub Actions CI blocks merge if Lighthouse accessibility score below 95 or axe-core finds WCAG 2.1 AA violations. GDPR compliance: Matomo self-hosted with IP anonymization (last 2 octets zeroed), no cross-site cookies, no personal data stored; no cookie consent banner required under ePrivacy Directive exemption for anonymized analytics.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Netlify project connected to GitHub repository via webhook. Feature branches automatically generate deploy preview URLs visible in PR comments. GitHub Actions PR workflow: Eleventy build validation, Pagefind index generation, Lighthouse CI (performance > 90, accessibility > 95, best-practices > 90, seo > 90), axe-core CLI scan (--tags wcag2a,wcag2aa --exit) on home, report library, report detail, author, and press release page types -- all checks are required status checks blocking merge. Merge to main triggers Netlify production deploy (average build time 90 seconds for 200 reports, monitored against 3-minute budget). Git LFS quota monitored monthly; Netlify Large Media bandwidth tracked in Netlify dashboard.
target_output.observability: Netlify build logs accessible in Netlify dashboard and GitHub Actions run summaries. Netlify Analytics (built-in) tracks bandwidth consumption, 404 error rates, and top-requested asset paths without personal data. Matomo self-hosted analytics tracks page views by country and thematic area category, PDF download events keyed by report slug, and referral sources. Lighthouse CI trend reports in GitHub Actions artifact storage track accessibility score history across deployments. Build time graphed in GitHub Actions summaries; team alerted via Slack if build exceeds 180 seconds.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Netlify free tier covers static site hosting for a public NGO (bandwidth up to 100 GB/month; build minutes up to 300/month). Git LFS storage at $5/month per 50 GB data pack for PDF assets. Matomo VPS estimated at $10/month (2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM sufficient for analytics at NGO traffic volumes). Total estimated monthly cost under $20, scaling to $50 at 500 GB monthly PDF download bandwidth.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Launch (6 weeks): Eleventy 2.0 build, Nunjucks report library template, Tailwind CSS self-hosted build, Pagefind search index, Netlify _headers CSP and security headers, GitHub Actions CI with Lighthouse and axe-core blocking checks, Git LFS PDF assets, Matomo analytics deployment, accessible report detail pages meeting WCAG 2.1 AA. Phase 2 (8 weeks post-launch): Author profile pages, press release archive, thematic area landing pages with curated featured reports, Eleventy Image srcset optimization for author photos. Phase 3 (16 weeks): Multilingual support (Spanish and French) via Eleventy i18n plugin, interactive deforestation trend charts using Observable Plot (no server API required), newsletter signup via Buttondown API integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: Nunjucks templates must use semantic HTML5 (article, section, nav, main, aside landmarks). All report download links follow accessible naming pattern. YAML frontmatter schema validated via Eleventy data cascade schema checks on PR. axe-core and Lighthouse CI are non-negotiable blocking gates -- accessibility failures are treated as build-breaking bugs. PDF assets must meet tagged PDF structure requirement before upload (editorial checklist item). Tailwind CSS purge configured to scan all Nunjucks templates; unused utility classes not shipped. No inline style attributes in templates.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Pagefind index build time exceeds 3-minute budget as report library grows beyond 500 entries with large YAML frontmatter -- mitigate by configuring Pagefind to index only report title and abstract fields rather than full body content. Risk: Git LFS storage costs grow with annual report archives -- mitigate by evaluating Netlify Large Media CDN offload and setting a per-PDF size limit of 50 MB in the editorial upload checklist. Tradeoff: Eleventy over Hugo for template flexibility -- Eleventy Nunjucks inheritance is more expressive for the nested report layout hierarchy, accepting slower build times compared to Hugo.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Pagefind over Lunr.js for build-time search (Pagefind more current and maintained). Confirmed Matomo self-hosted over Plausible Cloud (self-hosted gives the NGO direct data control under GDPR). Chose Git LFS with Netlify Large Media over S3 for PDF assets (single-repo content workflow for editorial team). Tailwind CSS self-hosted build confirmed over Tailwind CDN link to satisfy CSP self-only style-src requirement.

metadata.notes: Round 2 NGO static site fixes missing CSP headers (ISS-001) and absent accessibility CI automation (ISS-002); Eleventy + Pagefind + Netlify architecture.

project_goal.value: Migrate a utility company legacy on-premise smart meter data ingestion system to a cloud-native streaming architecture processing high-frequency electricity consumption readings from millions of residential meters, with zero billing data loss during cutover
project_goal.rationale: Core modernization objective to replace a monolithic on-premise ingest system with an elastic cloud streaming pipeline

target_users.value: Billing engine, demand forecasting models, and grid operations analytics systems consuming normalized meter consumption data
target_users.rationale: Internal downstream consumers only; no direct end-user access to the pipeline

project_class.rationale: High-scale streaming data ingestion and processing pipeline; no user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: Streaming ingestion, windowed aggregation, batch reconciliation, and analytics query support

complexity_level.rationale: Millions of residential meters generating high-frequency readings require sub-second Kinesis throughput and Flink windowed aggregation at scale

risk_level.rationale: Residential electricity consumption data is personal data under GDPR/CCPA; NERC CIP applies as critical infrastructure; billing data loss has direct revenue impact

data_sensitivity.rationale: Electricity consumption readings reveal home occupancy patterns and behavior -- personal data under GDPR and CCPA

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline is internal infrastructure consumed only by other internal systems; no external API surface

access_model.value: IAM role-based access for downstream consumers (billing engine, forecasting, analytics); Cognito identity pool for internal operator tooling; no external access
access_model.rationale: AWS IAM enforces least-privilege access to Kinesis streams and ClickHouse query endpoints

feature_scope.value: Kinesis Data Streams ingestion from smart meter telemetry collectors, Flink windowed aggregation (15-min tumbling windows for billing, 1-min sliding windows for anomaly detection), ClickHouse analytical query layer, dual-write migration phase, output checksum reconciliation per billing_cycle, GDPR right-to-erasure for meter_id to household mapping
feature_scope.rationale: Full pipeline feature set for billing engine replacement

mvp_scope.value: Kinesis ingestion for 10% of meter fleet, Flink 15-min tumbling window aggregation, ClickHouse write path, dual-write parallel run against legacy system for one billing cycle
mvp_scope.rationale: 10% fleet rollout validates throughput and accuracy before full cutover

security_baseline.value: KMS encryption for Kinesis streams and ClickHouse data at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, IAM least-privilege, VPC private subnets for all pipeline components, no meter data in application logs
security_baseline.rationale: GDPR and NERC CIP require encryption at rest and in transit for residential energy consumption data

frontend_stack.rationale: No user-facing browser UI; operator access via AWS Console and internal Grafana dashboards

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Flink on Amazon EMR Serverless, Kinesis Data Streams for ingestion
backend_stack.rationale: Flink on EMR Serverless provides elastic scale for variable meter fleet sizes; Kinesis is the AWS-native streaming backbone

data_platform.value: ClickHouse (self-managed on EC2) for analytical queries, Redis 7 for deduplication and Flink state coordination, S3 for Flink checkpoints and cold archival
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse sub-second aggregation queries for billing engine; Redis for idempotent deduplication of meter reading retransmissions

hosting_target.value: AWS: EMR Serverless for Flink, Kinesis Data Streams, ClickHouse on EC2 r6i in us-east-1, ElastiCache Redis 7, S3 for checkpoints and archive
hosting_target.rationale: EMR Serverless eliminates Flink cluster management; ClickHouse on EC2 gives control over cluster sizing for query performance

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw meter readings retained 13 months for GDPR billing dispute resolution; aggregated consumption data retained 7 years for regulatory reporting; GDPR right to erasure removes household-to-meter_id mapping within 30 days of request
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 17 erasure rights apply; 13-month raw retention supports billing dispute resolution window

future_scope.value: Full meter fleet cutover, demand response real-time alerting, ML-based anomaly detection on Flink streaming, customer energy analytics portal
future_scope.rationale: Post-cutover value-add analytics on top of the new streaming foundation

constraints.value: Zero billing data loss during cutover; dual-write phase must validate checksum equality per billing_cycle before legacy shutdown; NERC CIP requires air-gapped network segment for critical infrastructure components; budget under $15000/month at full fleet scale
constraints.rationale: Billing data loss is a regulatory and revenue risk; NERC CIP applies as grid infrastructure

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for Kinesis and EMR metrics, Grafana + Prometheus for Flink job metrics, custom dashboard for dual-write reconciliation status and billing_cycle checksum comparison
observability_baseline.rationale: Migration-phase reconciliation dashboard is critical for cutover decision-making

execution_preference.value: Phased rollout: 10% fleet pilot, 50% fleet validation, 100% fleet cutover with legacy shutdown
execution_preference.rationale: Phased approach reduces cutover risk for critical infrastructure

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 5 (data minimization), Article 17 (right to erasure for household-meter_id mapping), CCPA right-to-deletion for California customers, NERC CIP-003 through CIP-011 for cyber security of bulk electric system data
compliance_context.rationale: Residential consumption data is personal data; grid infrastructure subject to NERC CIP; California utility customers have CCPA rights

project.goal: Migrate legacy on-premise smart meter ingest to cloud-native Kinesis/Flink/ClickHouse pipeline
project.target_users: Billing engine, demand forecasting, grid operations analytics

backend.framework: Apache Flink on EMR Serverless, Kinesis Data Streams
backend.aggregation: 15-min tumbling windows for billing, 1-min sliding windows for anomaly detection

security.auth: AWS IAM roles for downstream consumers, Cognito for operator tooling

devops.hosting: EMR Serverless, Kinesis, EC2 ClickHouse cluster

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 4 revision is converging on the critical remaining issue: the Flink checkpoint consistency model during the dual-write phase. If Flink checkpoints on EMR Serverless fail during the parallel run, the billing_cycle checksum comparison will show false discrepancies, triggering unnecessary rollbacks. The checkpoint failure recovery path must be explicitly modeled in the plan. The billing engine SLA dependency on ClickHouse p99 query latency must also be quantified -- previous rounds left this as a vague requirement.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The core issue from ISS-003 -- Flink checkpoint S3 path isolation per environment -- has been correctly addressed in the previous round. The remaining ISS-004 issue about ClickHouse replication lag during peak meter ingestion is the last blocking concern. At full fleet scale (10M+ meters), Kinesis shard count must be sized to handle peak morning consumption reporting without shard-level throughput throttling. Previous plans under-specified the Kinesis resharding strategy for the 50%-to-100% fleet transition.
reasoner_reviews.security: NERC CIP network segmentation was added in round 3 and is correctly reflected. The GDPR right-to-erasure path for household-meter_id decoupling needs explicit documentation in the security section -- previous rounds mentioned it but did not specify the erasure mechanism in ClickHouse (mutation-based DELETE). CCPA deletion for California customers must have a distinct request tracking workflow.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Budget at full fleet scale must be recalculated with ClickHouse cluster sizing for 10M meters. Previous round estimated $12000/month but did not account for S3 Flink checkpoint storage at high checkpoint frequency. Kinesis shard cost at full fleet is the largest variable.

backend.service_design: Flink streaming application on EMR Serverless with three job graphs: (1) MeterIngestJob: Kinesis source, deduplication via Redis idempotency keys (meter_id + reading_timestamp), writes raw consumption_reading to ClickHouse. (2) BillingAggregationJob: reads from ClickHouse raw readings, 15-min tumbling window sum per smart_meter, writes to aggregation_window table. (3) AnomalyDetectionJob: 1-min sliding window per meter, z-score deviation check, writes anomaly_event records.
backend.failure_handling: Kinesis shard iterator expiry handled by Flink Kinesis consumer with automatic shard reassignment. Flink checkpoint failure on S3: EMR Serverless job restarts from last successful checkpoint; checkpoint paths are environment-isolated (/prod/flink-checkpoints/ vs /staging/). ClickHouse write failures trigger Flink backpressure; undeliverable records held in Kinesis for up to 24 hours (stream retention). Redis deduplication key TTL 48h covers meter retransmission windows.
backend.scaling_notes: Kinesis shard count: 1 shard per 1MB/s ingestion. At 10M meters transmitting every 15 min (avg 200 bytes/reading): peak throughput ~2.2MB/s per cohort group, target 10 shards at 10% fleet, 50 shards at 50%, 500 shards at full fleet. EMR Serverless Flink auto-scales worker units based on Kinesis lag metric. ClickHouse cluster: r6i.4xlarge x 3 nodes at full fleet for sub-100ms p99 billing query latency.
backend.background_jobs: DualWriteReconciliationJob (scheduled per billing_cycle_end): reads aggregated totals from cloud ClickHouse and legacy system, computes checksum per billing_cycle, writes comparison to migration_batch table with pass/fail status. MeterFleetRolloutJob: batch Airflow DAG managing meter_id assignment to cloud pipeline in cohorts (10%, 50%, 100%). GDPRErasureJob: nightly batch processing erasure requests, running ClickHouse ALTER TABLE DELETE WHERE meter_id = X and removing household-meter_id mapping from Redis.

security.auth_design: IAM roles for downstream consumers: billing-engine-role (ClickHouse read on consumption_reading, aggregation_window), forecasting-role (ClickHouse read on aggregation_window), ops-role (Kinesis GetRecords for monitoring). Cognito identity pool for internal operator Grafana dashboards.
security.authorization_model: IAM least-privilege: Kinesis PutRecord restricted to meter telemetry collector service account; ClickHouse roles restrict billing engine to SELECT only on billing-relevant tables; no cross-consumer data access.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for ClickHouse admin credentials and Redis AUTH token. KMS customer-managed keys for Kinesis stream encryption and S3 Flink checkpoint bucket encryption. 30-day rotation.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail for all AWS API calls. Flink job logs in CloudWatch with meter_id masked. ClickHouse access logs with query_id, user_role, tables_accessed. GDPR erasure requests tracked in erasure_request table with status and completion timestamp.

data.entities: smart_meter (meter_id VARCHAR PK, tariff_zone_id FK, install_date, status ENUM active/decommissioned, household_mapping_id -- nullable after GDPR erasure). consumption_reading (id UUID, meter_id VARCHAR, reading_kwh NUMERIC, reading_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, quality_flag ENUM valid/estimated/missing, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, source ENUM cloud_pipeline/legacy_dual_write). aggregation_window (meter_id, window_start TIMESTAMPTZ, window_end TIMESTAMPTZ, window_type ENUM 15min/1hour/daily, total_kwh NUMERIC, reading_count INT). tariff_zone (id, name, region, rate_schedule). billing_cycle (id, meter_id, period_start, period_end, total_kwh_cloud, total_kwh_legacy, checksum_match BOOL, reconciled_at). anomaly_event (id, meter_id, detected_at, deviation_zscore, window_kwh, expected_kwh, status ENUM open/investigated/closed). migration_batch (id, fleet_pct INT, start_date, cutover_status ENUM running/validated/cutover_complete).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse 3-node cluster: consumption_reading table with MergeTree engine partitioned by toYYYYMM(reading_timestamp), ORDER BY (meter_id, reading_timestamp). aggregation_window with AggregatingMergeTree. S3-backed Flink checkpoints partitioned by environment. ElastiCache Redis 7 for deduplication keys (meter_id:reading_timestamp, TTL 48h) and Flink operator state coordination. S3 Parquet archive for consumption_reading older than 13 months.
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw consumption_reading retained 13 months in ClickHouse hot storage, then exported to S3 Parquet. GDPR/CCPA erasure: ALTER TABLE consumption_reading DELETE WHERE meter_id = {id} in ClickHouse (mutation, async); household_mapping_id set to NULL in smart_meter. Erasure completion logged to erasure_request table.

devops.deployment_topology: Kinesis Data Streams in VPC private subnet (NERC CIP air-gapped segment for production). EMR Serverless Flink application in same VPC. ClickHouse cluster (r6i.4xlarge x3) in private subnet with internal NLB. ElastiCache Redis 7 in private subnet. No public internet access to any pipeline component. Bastion host for operator access.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, pytest unit tests (LocalStackS3 + Redis Testcontainers), Flink job JAR build, Docker push to ECR. Deployment: Terraform-managed Kinesis shard count, EMR Serverless job update via AWS CLI, ClickHouse schema migrations via clickhouse-migrations tool. Blue-green Flink job deployment: start new job version consuming from same Kinesis stream before stopping old job.
devops.observability_stack: Grafana + Prometheus: Flink job metrics (records_in, records_out, checkpoint_duration, backpressure_ratio), Kinesis GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds (consumer lag), ClickHouse query_log p50/p95/p99 latency, dual_write_checksum_match_rate. CloudWatch alarms: Kinesis IteratorAge > 60s, Flink checkpoint failure, ClickHouse disk utilization > 80%.

ISS-001.title: Flink checkpoint S3 path not environment-isolated
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan used a shared S3 path for Flink checkpoints across all environments; staging job restores could corrupt production checkpoint state.

ISS-002.title: NERC CIP network segmentation not specified
ISS-002.detail: Early rounds did not address NERC CIP requirement for air-gapped network segment for critical infrastructure pipeline components.

ISS-003.title: GDPR erasure path not modeled for ClickHouse
ISS-003.detail: Previous plans reference GDPR right-to-erasure but do not specify the ClickHouse mutation-based DELETE mechanism or the household-meter_id decoupling path. Erasure completion tracking is absent.

ISS-004.title: Kinesis shard count under-specified for full fleet scale
ISS-004.detail: Current plan does not quantify Kinesis shard requirements at 10M meter full-fleet scale or provide a resharding strategy for the 50%-to-100% fleet transition.

focus_issues.title: GDPR erasure path not modeled for ClickHouse
focus_issues.detail: ClickHouse mutation DELETE mechanism and household-meter_id decoupling not specified. Erasure request tracking absent.

focus_issues.title: Kinesis shard count under-specified for full fleet scale
focus_issues.detail: Shard count calculation and resharding strategy for 50%-to-100% fleet transition not documented.

latest_recommendations: Specify ClickHouse ALTER TABLE DELETE mutation for GDPR erasure with async completion tracking in erasure_request table
Quantify Kinesis shard count formula at full fleet scale and document resharding procedure for 50%-to-100% fleet transition
Add ClickHouse p99 query latency SLA quantification for billing engine dependency

previous_plan.title: Smart Meter Streaming Migration Pipeline - Round 3
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Kinesis + Flink + ClickHouse streaming pipeline replacing a legacy on-premise smart meter ingest system. Dual-write parallel run with billing_cycle checksum reconciliation validates accuracy before fleet cutover. NERC CIP VPC isolation and GDPR-compliant retention policies applied.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Kinesis Data Streams in VPC private subnet (NERC CIP compliant) ingests meter readings. Flink on EMR Serverless processes 15-min tumbling windows for billing aggregation and 1-min sliding windows for anomaly detection. ClickHouse cluster on EC2 r6i stores normalized consumption readings and aggregation windows. Dual-write ReconciliationJob compares cloud and legacy pipeline totals per billing_cycle. GDPR erasure rights acknowledged but implementation deferred.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: KMS encryption for Kinesis and S3. TLS 1.3 in transit. IAM least-privilege roles per downstream consumer. Flink checkpoint S3 paths isolated per environment (/prod/flink-checkpoints/). VPC private subnets with no public internet access per NERC CIP requirements. GDPR right-to-erasure for household-meter_id mentioned but ClickHouse deletion mechanism not specified.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI with ruff, pytest, Flink JAR build. Terraform for Kinesis and EMR Serverless. Blue-green Flink job deployment. Grafana + Prometheus for Flink and Kinesis metrics. CloudWatch alarms for consumer lag and checkpoint failures.

best_plan.title: Smart Meter Streaming Migration Pipeline - Round 3
best_plan.executive_summary: A Kinesis + Flink + ClickHouse streaming pipeline replacing a legacy on-premise smart meter ingest system. Dual-write parallel run with billing_cycle checksum reconciliation validates accuracy before fleet cutover.
best_plan.architecture_overview: Kinesis Data Streams in VPC private subnet ingests meter readings. Flink on EMR Serverless processes billing aggregation windows. ClickHouse cluster stores consumption readings. Dual-write reconciliation validates before cutover.
best_plan.security_and_compliance: KMS encryption, TLS 1.3, IAM least-privilege, NERC CIP VPC isolation, environment-isolated Flink checkpoint paths.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 revision addressing two remaining issues: ISS-003 (GDPR erasure path in ClickHouse -- needs ALTER TABLE DELETE mutation mechanism with async tracking) and ISS-004 (Kinesis shard count formula and resharding strategy for fleet transition). Both issues are well-understood from previous audit feedback. The fix is primarily in the security_and_compliance section (ISS-003) and the scaling_notes / deployment sections (ISS-004). I also need to add ClickHouse p99 query latency SLA for billing engine as recommended by auditors.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit ClickHouse ALTER TABLE consumption_reading DELETE WHERE meter_id = {id} mutation mechanism to security_and_compliance section. Added erasure_request table to data_model for tracking GDPR/CCPA erasure request status and completion timestamps. Documented household-meter_id decoupling (setting household_mapping_id to NULL in smart_meter table). Added GDPRErasureJob batch processor to the backend service design.
fix_report.expected_outcome: GDPR and CCPA erasure requests now have a fully specified execution path: ALTER TABLE mutation in ClickHouse, Redis deduplication key expiry, smart_meter.household_mapping_id nullification, and erasure_request status tracking. Resolves ISS-003.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Kinesis shard count derivation formula to cost_and_scaling and deployment_and_operations sections: 1 shard per 1MB/s throughput; at 10M meters transmitting every 15 min at 200 bytes/reading = peak 2.2MB/s per 1M meters = 22 shards per million meters = 220 shards at full fleet. Documented resharding procedure using UpdateShardCount API in two steps (10 shards to 110 at 50% fleet, 110 to 220 at 100% fleet) with 24h stabilization window between steps.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Kinesis shard sizing is now quantified from first principles with a reproducible formula. The resharding procedure for 50%-to-100% fleet transition is explicitly documented with UpdateShardCount API steps and stabilization windows. Resolves ISS-004.

target_output.title: Smart Meter Streaming Migration Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A Kinesis Data Streams + Apache Flink on EMR Serverless + ClickHouse pipeline migrating a utility company legacy on-premise smart meter ingest system to cloud-native streaming. Processes high-frequency electricity consumption readings from up to 10 million residential meters with 15-minute tumbling windows for billing and 1-minute sliding windows for anomaly detection. Dual-write parallel run with per-billing_cycle checksum reconciliation validates zero data loss before fleet cutover. GDPR and CCPA erasure requests processed via ClickHouse ALTER TABLE DELETE mutation with async tracking. NERC CIP network segmentation enforced via VPC private subnets with no public internet exposure.
target_output.architecture_overview: Five-layer streaming architecture on AWS us-east-1 in a NERC CIP-compliant VPC with no public internet access. Layer 1 (Ingestion): Kinesis Data Streams sharded at 1 shard per 1MB/s throughput target (22 shards at 10% fleet, scaling to 220 at full 10M-meter fleet). Layer 2 (Stream Processing): Flink on EMR Serverless with three job graphs -- MeterIngestJob (deduplication via Redis, write to ClickHouse), BillingAggregationJob (15-min tumbling window sum per meter), AnomalyDetectionJob (1-min sliding window z-score). Layer 3 (Analytical Storage): ClickHouse 3-node EC2 r6i cluster with MergeTree partitioned by reading month, targeting < 100ms p99 billing query latency. Layer 4 (Migration Control): Dual-write parallel run with DualWriteReconciliationJob computing checksums per billing_cycle against legacy system output. Layer 5 (Compliance): GDPRErasureJob processing ALTER TABLE DELETE mutations for erasure requests; CCPA deletion request tracking workflow.
target_output.technology_stack: Streaming: Kinesis Data Streams (220 shards at full fleet). Processing: Apache Flink 1.18 on EMR Serverless, Python 3.12 via PyFlink, GDAL for geospatial utilities. Storage: ClickHouse 23.8 LTS on EC2 r6i.4xlarge (3-node cluster). Cache/State: ElastiCache Redis 7 (deduplication and Flink state coordination). Archival: S3 Parquet with lifecycle to Glacier after 13 months. Orchestration: Apache Airflow on MWAA for batch reconciliation and erasure DAGs. CI: GitHub Actions + Terraform. Monitoring: Grafana, Prometheus, CloudWatch.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Meter Reading Ingestion: Kinesis consumers ingest telemetry collector readings with Redis-based idempotent deduplication on meter_id + reading_timestamp. Billing Aggregation: Flink 15-min tumbling window sums consumption per meter, writes aggregation_window records consumed by billing engine. Anomaly Detection: Flink 1-min sliding window z-score deviation flags anomaly_event records for grid operations. Dual-Write Reconciliation: nightly DualWriteReconciliationJob compares cloud vs legacy billing_cycle totals, writes checksum match result to migration_batch table. GDPR Erasure: GDPRErasureJob runs nightly, executes ClickHouse ALTER TABLE DELETE for pending erasure requests, nullifies household_mapping_id in smart_meter, records completion in erasure_request table. Fleet Rollout Management: MeterFleetRolloutJob assigns meter cohorts to cloud pipeline in controlled percentage increments.
target_output.system_components: Kinesis Data Streams (VPC private, KMS-encrypted, 220 shards at full fleet), EMR Serverless Flink application (MeterIngestJob, BillingAggregationJob, AnomalyDetectionJob), ClickHouse 3-node EC2 r6i.4xlarge cluster (internal NLB, private subnet), ElastiCache Redis 7 (deduplication keys and Flink state), S3 Flink checkpoints bucket (per-environment paths: /prod/flink-checkpoints/, /staging/), S3 Parquet archive bucket (consumption_reading > 13 months), MWAA Airflow (reconciliation and erasure DAGs), CloudWatch + Grafana + Prometheus (monitoring), Bastion host (operator access per NERC CIP).
target_output.workflows: Meter Reading Ingestion Workflow: Telemetry collector pushes reading {meter_id, reading_kwh, reading_timestamp} to Kinesis. Flink MeterIngestJob consumer reads shard, checks Redis deduplication key meter_id:reading_timestamp (TTL 48h). Duplicate: discard. New record: INSERT INTO consumption_reading in ClickHouse with quality_flag=valid; set Redis key with TTL. Billing Aggregation: BillingAggregationJob emits 15-min window aggregates to aggregation_window table; billing engine queries ClickHouse SELECT sum(total_kwh) WHERE meter_id IN (...) AND window_start BETWEEN ... for sub-100ms p99 response. Dual-Write Reconciliation: At billing_cycle_end, DualWriteReconciliationJob queries ClickHouse total_kwh and legacy system total_kwh for each meter in the cohort; SHA256 checksum computed over sorted (meter_id, total_kwh) pairs; match recorded in migration_batch.checksum_match. If mismatch > 0.01% of meters: cutover blocked, PagerDuty alert. GDPR Erasure: Resident submits erasure request; erasure_request record created with status=pending. GDPRErasureJob nightly batch: for each pending request, executes ClickHouse ALTER TABLE consumption_reading DELETE WHERE meter_id = {id} (async mutation), sets smart_meter.household_mapping_id = NULL, expires Redis deduplication keys for meter_id, updates erasure_request.status = completed with completion_timestamp. CCPA requests follow same path with separate ccpa_erasure_request table for California regulatory audit trail.
target_output.data_model: smart_meter (meter_id VARCHAR PK, tariff_zone_id UUID FK, install_date DATE, status ENUM active/decommissioned, household_mapping_id UUID NULLABLE -- set NULL on GDPR erasure). consumption_reading (id UUID, meter_id VARCHAR, reading_kwh NUMERIC, reading_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, quality_flag ENUM valid/estimated/missing, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, source ENUM cloud_pipeline/legacy_dual_write) -- ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(reading_timestamp) ORDER BY (meter_id, reading_timestamp). aggregation_window (meter_id VARCHAR, window_start TIMESTAMPTZ, window_end TIMESTAMPTZ, window_type ENUM 15min/1hour/daily, total_kwh NUMERIC, reading_count INT) -- ClickHouse AggregatingMergeTree. tariff_zone (id UUID, name VARCHAR, region VARCHAR, rate_schedule JSONB). billing_cycle (id UUID, meter_id VARCHAR, period_start DATE, period_end DATE, total_kwh_cloud NUMERIC, total_kwh_legacy NUMERIC, checksum_match BOOL, reconciled_at TIMESTAMPTZ). anomaly_event (id UUID, meter_id VARCHAR, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, deviation_zscore NUMERIC, window_kwh NUMERIC, expected_kwh NUMERIC, status ENUM open/investigated/closed). migration_batch (id UUID, fleet_pct INT, start_date DATE, meter_count INT, cutover_status ENUM running/validated/cutover_complete). erasure_request (id UUID, meter_id VARCHAR, request_source ENUM gdpr/ccpa, requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE).
target_output.api_design: Internal-only interfaces. Billing engine: direct ClickHouse query via internal NLB. Flink management: EMR Serverless REST API for job start/stop/status (AWS SDK, IAM-authenticated). Kinesis: PutRecords API from telemetry collectors (IAM role). Airflow DAG triggers: MWAA REST API for manual reconciliation trigger. Operator access: Grafana dashboards via internal ALB + Cognito identity pool authentication. No public API surface.
target_output.security_and_compliance: NERC CIP compliance: all pipeline components in VPC private subnets with no public internet access; bastion host for operator SSH; CloudTrail logging all AWS API calls. KMS CMKs for Kinesis stream encryption and S3 bucket encryption at rest. TLS 1.3 enforced for all internal service communication. IAM least-privilege: billing-engine-role has SELECT-only on ClickHouse consumption_reading and aggregation_window via internal NLB; no cross-consumer table access. GDPR erasure (ISS-003 resolution): GDPRErasureJob executes ClickHouse ALTER TABLE consumption_reading DELETE WHERE meter_id = {id} -- this is a ClickHouse asynchronous mutation; erasure_request.status updated to in_progress at mutation submit and polled to completed when system.mutations confirms completion. smart_meter.household_mapping_id set to NULL in PostgreSQL metadata store, severing household identity link. Redis deduplication keys for meter_id pattern-deleted. CCPA requests tracked in ccpa_erasure_request table with California-specific 45-day completion SLA. Erasure completion logged to erasure_request for GDPR Article 30 records-of-processing audit. GDPR data minimization: meter_id is pseudonymous; household identity stored only in smart_meter.household_mapping_id which is erasable independently of consumption data.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Kinesis shard sizing (ISS-004 resolution): formula = ceil(peak_throughput_bytes_per_sec / 1000000). At full 10M meter fleet: 10M meters x 200 bytes/reading / 900 seconds (15-min interval) = 2222 bytes/s per meter aggregate = 2.2MB/s per million meters = 22 shards/million meters. Full fleet: 220 shards. Resharding procedure: Step 1 at 50% fleet (110 shards): aws kinesis update-shard-count --shard-count 110 --scaling-type UNIFORM_SCALING; wait 24h for consumer group rebalancing before routing 50% fleet traffic. Step 2 at 100% fleet (220 shards): repeat UpdateShardCount to 220 with 24h stabilization window. GitHub Actions pipeline: ruff lint, pytest + Testcontainers ClickHouse + Redis, Flink JAR build, Terraform plan. Deployment: Terraform apply for Kinesis shard changes; EMR Serverless job update with blue-green (start new job version consuming Kinesis before stopping old version). MWAA DAG sync on merge to main. ClickHouse schema changes via clickhouse-migrations with dry-run in staging first. Environments: dev (10 shards, 1-node ClickHouse), staging (22 shards, 3-node ClickHouse, full security controls), production (220 shards at full fleet, 3-node ClickHouse r6i.4xlarge). Rollback: Kinesis consumer group can be reverted to legacy pipeline within minutes by pointing meter telemetry collectors back to on-premise ingest.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards on Prometheus: kinesis_iterator_age_ms (consumer lag, alert > 60s), flink_records_in_per_second (ingestion throughput), flink_checkpoint_duration_ms (alert > 30s), clickhouse_query_p99_latency_ms (billing engine SLA: alert > 100ms), dual_write_checksum_mismatch_count (alert > 0), erasure_request_pending_count (SLA: < 30 days for GDPR). CloudWatch alarms: Kinesis ThrottledRecords > 0 (triggers resharding review), EMR Serverless job failure (PagerDuty). Fleet migration dashboard: percentage of meters on cloud pipeline vs legacy, reconciliation pass rate per billing_cycle. GDPR erasure SLA tracking: pending erasure requests older than 25 days trigger compliance team alert.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Full fleet cost estimate: Kinesis Data Streams 220 shards (~$2400/month), EMR Serverless Flink autoscale to 0 between windows (~$1800/month for continuous window processing), ClickHouse 3x r6i.4xlarge EC2 (~$4200/month Reserved Instances), ElastiCache Redis r6g.large (~$300/month), S3 checkpoint + archive (~$200/month), MWAA (~$300/month), CloudWatch + Grafana (~$200/month). Total at full fleet: ~$9400/month, within $15000/month budget. Kinesis is the primary cost lever: reduce shard count between 2am-4am low-traffic window via scheduled UpdateShardCount to save ~$400/month. ClickHouse Reserved Instance pricing saves 40% vs on-demand.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Pilot (12 weeks): 10% fleet (1M meters), 22 Kinesis shards, MeterIngestJob and BillingAggregationJob Flink jobs, ClickHouse 3-node cluster, dual-write ReconciliationJob, one billing_cycle checksum validation, GDPR erasure GDPRErasureJob, NERC CIP VPC isolation, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 Expansion (8 weeks): 50% fleet rollout (110 shards, UpdateShardCount step 1), AnomalyDetectionJob Flink job, CCPA erasure request workflow, Grafana SLA dashboards, billing engine ClickHouse query optimization. Phase 3 Full Cutover (6 weeks): 100% fleet (220 shards, UpdateShardCount step 2), 3-billing-cycle checksum validation before legacy shutdown, legacy system decommission plan, S3 Parquet archival for pre-migration historical data import.
target_output.development_guidelines: PyFlink 1.18 with Python 3.12; pin Flink version in requirements.txt. Flink jobs must be idempotent: Redis deduplication enforced at ingest, consumption_reading INSERT uses ReplacingMergeTree dedup on (meter_id, reading_timestamp). ClickHouse mutations (ALTER TABLE DELETE) are asynchronous -- always poll system.mutations.is_done before marking erasure_request as completed. Kinesis shard count changes via UpdateShardCount require 24h consumer rebalancing window; never reroute new traffic to resharded stream until Flink consumer group reports all partitions assigned. pytest unit tests use Testcontainers for ClickHouse and Redis. Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB locking; separate workspaces per environment.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Kinesis shard throttling during 50%-to-100% fleet transition before UpdateShardCount completes. Mitigation: 24h stabilization window, monitor ThrottledRecords CloudWatch metric, CloudWatch alarm triggers hold on fleet rollout if throttling detected. Risk: ClickHouse ALTER TABLE DELETE mutation backlog at large scale (erasure queue of 10000+ requests). Mitigation: GDPRErasureJob processes in batches of 100 with mutation completion polling; GDPR 30-day SLA provides adequate processing time. Risk: Billing_cycle checksum mismatch from clock skew between cloud and legacy pipeline. Mitigation: reconciliation window extends 15 minutes past billing_cycle_end to absorb reading transmission delays. Tradeoff: ClickHouse on EC2 over AWS ClickHouse-compatible managed service (Altinity) for cost control and cluster configuration flexibility; operational overhead managed by on-call runbooks. Risk: Flink EMR Serverless job cold start latency (30-60s) for Kinesis consumer lag spikes. Mitigation: keep minimum 2 Flink workers warm during peak hours via EMR Serverless initial capacity configuration.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: GDPR erasure mechanism for ClickHouse confirmed as ALTER TABLE DELETE async mutation with erasure_request status polling (ISS-003 resolved). Kinesis shard count formula established at 22 shards per million meters from first principles; resharding via UpdateShardCount with 24h stabilization (ISS-004 resolved). ClickHouse p99 billing query SLA set at < 100ms enforced via Grafana alert. Dual-write reconciliation mismatch threshold set at 0.01% of meters per billing_cycle before cutover is blocked.

metadata.notes: Round 4 revision resolving GDPR ClickHouse erasure mechanism (ISS-003) and Kinesis shard count formula (ISS-004). High-scale smart meter streaming migration with dual-write reconciliation and NERC CIP compliance.

profile.domain: Computational Ecology - GPU Species Distribution Modeling

project_goal.value: Research prototype for computational ecologists testing GPU-accelerated species distribution models using climate scenario projections, evaluating convergence speed and spatial prediction accuracy across multiple algorithm implementations.
project_goal.rationale: Proof-of-concept benchmarking infrastructure for GPU SDM research paper.

target_users.value: Computational ecologists and PhD researchers in the lab running HPC cluster jobs to benchmark SDM algorithm variants against climate projection datasets.
target_users.rationale: Small research team of 3-5 users on shared HPC cluster.

project_class.rationale: Experimental benchmarking system; not production-hardened, prioritizes scientific reproducibility.

capabilities.rationale: GPU training jobs, DuckDB result storage, and Weights and Biases analytics logging.

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-GPU PyTorch training, Slurm job orchestration, and multi-metric evaluation pipeline require advanced engineering.

risk_level.rationale: Internal research use; no personal data; primary risk is reproducibility failure, not security breach.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Species occurrence and climate projection datasets are public scientific data.

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on HPC cluster with no public network exposure; Weights and Biases cloud logging is opt-in.

access_model.value: Shared HPC cluster with Slurm job submission; researchers use their own HPC accounts; no additional access control layer.
access_model.rationale: Access governed by existing HPC cluster authentication.

feature_scope.value: Multi-algorithm SDM training on GPU (MaxEnt-GPU, BRT-GPU, BIOCLIM-GPU variants), GBIF species occurrence data ingestion, climate scenario layer ingestion (CHELSA, WorldClim), evaluation metrics (AUC, TSS, Boyce Index), Slurm batch job templates, W&B experiment tracking, DuckDB result aggregation.
feature_scope.rationale: Full benchmarking pipeline for the research paper.

mvp_scope.value: Single species, single climate scenario, MaxEnt-GPU vs BRT-GPU comparison with AUC and TSS metrics logged to Weights and Biases.
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP validates GPU training pipeline before multi-species and multi-scenario batch experiments.

security_baseline.value: HPC cluster user account isolation; no secrets beyond W&B API key in user environment variable; no external ports opened.
security_baseline.rationale: Research prototype on private HPC; minimal security requirements.

frontend_stack.value: None (no UI; results accessed via W&B dashboard and DuckDB queries in Jupyter notebooks).
frontend_stack.rationale: Research prototype uses existing analysis tools; no custom UI needed.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with PyTorch 2.3 and CUDA 12.1; scikit-learn for baseline metrics; GDAL for raster processing.
backend_stack.rationale: PyTorch GPU ecosystem standard for research ML; GDAL for species distribution raster operations.

data_platform.value: DuckDB embedded for aggregated benchmark results; HPC shared filesystem (Lustre) for raw occurrence CSVs and climate raster files.
data_platform.rationale: DuckDB enables fast analytical queries on benchmark results without a database server.

hosting_target.value: HPC cluster managed by Slurm workload manager with A100 GPU nodes.
hosting_target.rationale: Lab HPC cluster is the only GPU-equipped compute environment available.

privacy_retention_policy.value: All data is public scientific datasets; no personal data collected; benchmark result DuckDB files retained indefinitely for reproducibility.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Open scientific research; no data governance requirements beyond reproducibility.

future_scope.value: Multi-species batch experiments across all CHELSA SSP scenarios, ensemble prediction aggregation, automated hyperparameter sweep via W&B Sweeps.
future_scope.rationale: Extended experiments for journal paper submission.

constraints.value: Must run on the lab HPC cluster A100 nodes under Slurm; Python package installation via conda environment stored in shared filesystem; no root access.
constraints.rationale: HPC cluster policy constraints.

observability_baseline.value: Weights and Biases experiment tracking for all training runs; stderr/stdout captured in Slurm job log files.
observability_baseline.rationale: W&B standard for ML research experiment reproducibility.

execution_preference.value: Iterative research cycles with GitHub version control for code and configuration; reproducibility via pinned conda environment YAML.
execution_preference.rationale: Research workflow requiring reproducible experiments.

project.goal: GPU-accelerated SDM benchmarking prototype for computational ecology research
project.target_users: PhD researchers on shared HPC cluster
project.timeline: Running experiments for research paper in 10 weeks

backend.framework: PyTorch 2.3 + CUDA 12.1 + scikit-learn
backend.raster: GDAL 3.8 for climate layer and occurrence raster operations

security.auth: HPC Slurm user accounts
security.secrets: W&B API key in user shell environment variable

data.raw_data: HPC Lustre shared filesystem (CHELSA NetCDF, GBIF occurrence CSV)

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for pytest suite on CPU (no GPU in CI)

constraints.compute: A100 GPU nodes under Slurm; no root access
constraints.packages: conda environment in shared Lustre filesystem

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 3 must address the persistent ISS-001 issue around missing random seed management for reproducibility. Scientific reproducibility is the core quality requirement for a research prototype -- if two runs of the same configuration produce different AUC scores, the benchmarking results are unpublishable. The fix is concrete: a global seed fixture in PyTorch, NumPy, and Python random, logged as a W&B hyperparameter on every run.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The DuckDB schema for storing benchmark_results remains the weakest part of the data model across all three rounds. benchmark_result should capture the full algorithm configuration as a JSON column, not just algorithm_variant name, to enable post-hoc SQL queries grouping runs by hyperparameter value. The seed fix and DuckDB schema update can both land in this round.
reasoner_reviews.security: Research prototype on private HPC; no significant security concerns. W&B API key in shell environment variable is standard research practice and acceptable for this threat model.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The ISS-002 concern about missing evaluation_metric records for Boyce Index is reasonable for a weak-quality prototype. The evaluation script should write all three metrics (AUC, TSS, Boyce Index) to benchmark_results in the same DuckDB transaction to ensure no partial result sets accumulate.

backend.service_design: Slurm batch job templates: train_sdm.sh (single model run), batch_experiment.sh (multi-algorithm sweep). Python entry point: src/train.py --species {gbif_taxon_key} --scenario {chelsa_scenario} --algorithm {maxent_gpu|brt_gpu|bioclim_gpu} --seed {int}. All runs log to W&B via wandb.init().
backend.business_modules: OccurrenceLoader (GBIF CSV ingestion, train/test split by geographic blocking). ClimateLayerStack (CHELSA NetCDF ingestion, variable selection, spatial extent clip via GDAL). MaxEntGPU / BRTGPU / BioclimGPU (PyTorch model implementations). EvaluationEngine (AUC-ROC via sklearn, TSS, Boyce Index computed on held-out test occurrence set). ResultWriter (DuckDB insert of benchmark_result rows with full config JSON).
backend.failure_handling: Slurm job exit codes propagated to job array status. Failed training runs logged to W&B with error tag. DuckDB writes use transactions; partial inserts rolled back on exception.
backend.backend_risks: CUDA out-of-memory errors on large climate layer stacks (1 km global resolution); mitigated by spatial extent subsetting and batch size tuning per algorithm. GDAL raster reprojection can be bottleneck for large extents; pre-process climate layers to common CRS before training experiments.

security.auth_design: No application-level authentication. HPC Slurm cluster handles user isolation. W&B API key stored in user shell environment variable (WANDB_API_KEY); never hardcoded in source code.
security.secrets_management: W&B API key in environment variable only. conda environment YAML committed to Git without secrets.
security.security_risks: W&B API key visible to other users with read access to job stdout logs if printed accidentally -- ensure wandb.init() call does not log the API key value.

data.entities: species_record (taxon_key INT, gbif_occurrence_id BIGINT, decimal_latitude REAL, decimal_longitude REAL, coordinate_uncertainty_m REAL, observed_on DATE). climate_scenario (scenario_id TEXT, model TEXT, ssp TEXT, time_period TEXT, variable_list TEXT[]). model_run (run_id UUID, taxon_key INT, scenario_id TEXT, algorithm_variant TEXT, seed INT, wandb_run_id TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP). benchmark_result (result_id UUID, run_id UUID FK, metric_name TEXT, metric_value REAL, dataset_split TEXT ENUM train/test, computed_at TIMESTAMP). algorithm_variant (name TEXT, config_json TEXT). prediction_grid (grid_id UUID, run_id UUID FK, grid_resolution_km REAL, crs TEXT, prediction_array_path TEXT).
data.storage_design: DuckDB file per experiment batch on Lustre shared filesystem. Raw occurrence CSVs and CHELSA NetCDF files on Lustre under /data/sdm/. Prediction rasters written as GeoTIFF files alongside DuckDB for spatial analysis in QGIS.
data.data_risks: DuckDB file on Lustre shared filesystem can be corrupted by simultaneous writes from parallel Slurm job array tasks -- use separate DuckDB files per job and merge via DuckDB ATTACH after job array completion.

devops.deployment_topology: Slurm job scripts in jobs/ directory. conda environment defined in environment.yml; activated in job scripts. No containerization (HPC policy restriction).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions runs pytest on CPU-only test subset (mocked GPU calls via CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES='') on every PR. No GPU CI available; GPU integration tested manually on HPC before PR merge to main.
devops.observability_stack: W&B run page shows training loss, AUC curve, TSS and Boyce Index per epoch, convergence step count, and GPU memory utilization. Slurm job stdout/stderr logs per job ID in logs/ directory. benchmark_results DuckDB queryable in Jupyter notebook for cross-run comparison.

ISS-001.title: No global random seed management causing non-reproducible benchmark results
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 both lack explicit seeding of torch, numpy.random, and Python random modules before training. Two runs of identical configuration produce different AUC values, making results unpublishable.

history.note: Identified in round 1: no seed parameter in train.py.
Round 2 added --seed CLI arg but did not call torch.manual_seed or numpy.random.seed in the training loop.

ISS-002.title: benchmark_result missing Boyce Index metric; evaluation is incomplete
ISS-002.detail: EvaluationEngine in round 2 writes AUC and TSS to benchmark_results but does not compute or log Boyce Index, which is required for the presence-only SDM validation protocol used in the research paper.

history.note: Boyce Index absent from evaluation module.
EvaluationEngine refactored but Boyce Index computation still missing.

latest_recommendations: Implement seed_everything(seed) function calling torch.manual_seed, torch.cuda.manual_seed_all, numpy.random.seed, and random.seed; call it at the top of train.py before any model or data initialization; log seed value to W&B config.
Add Boyce Index computation to EvaluationEngine using the ecospat.boyce implementation; write metric_name=boyce_index row to benchmark_results in the same DuckDB transaction as AUC and TSS.

previous_plan.title: GPU SDM Benchmarking Prototype - Round 2
previous_plan.executive_summary: PyTorch 2.3 + CUDA 12.1 GPU training pipeline for benchmarking MaxEntGPU, BRTGPU, and BioclimGPU algorithm variants on CHELSA climate projections; Slurm job templates; DuckDB benchmark result storage; W&B experiment tracking.
previous_plan.data_model: species_record, climate_scenario, model_run, benchmark_result (AUC, TSS only), algorithm_variant, prediction_grid stored in DuckDB on Lustre.
previous_plan.workflows: Researcher submits Slurm batch job with taxon_key, scenario, algorithm, and seed; PyTorch trains model on A100; EvaluationEngine computes AUC and TSS; results written to DuckDB.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 is a targeted fix round for a weak-quality research prototype. ISS-001 requires completing the seed_everything() implementation with all four library calls (torch, torch.cuda, numpy.random, random) and logging seed to W&B config. ISS-002 requires adding Boyce Index computation to EvaluationEngine and writing it to DuckDB in the same transaction. These are concrete code-level fixes, not architectural changes. The plan quality remains weak overall because several sections are thin for this domain -- the architecture is research prototype scale, not production.

fix_report.action_taken: Added seed_everything(seed: int) function calling torch.manual_seed(seed), torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed), numpy.random.seed(seed), and random.seed(seed). Function called at line 1 of train.py main() before OccurrenceLoader and ClimateLayerStack initialization. Seed value logged to wandb.config on every run. Also set torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True and torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False for fully deterministic CUDA operations.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Identical --seed values produce byte-for-byte identical AUC, TSS, and Boyce Index values across two runs on the same GPU hardware; benchmark results publishable.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented Boyce Index in EvaluationEngine using manual ecospat formula (continuous Boyce index via moving window correlation between predicted suitability bins and observed occurrence density). Written to benchmark_results as metric_name='boyce_index' in the same DuckDB transaction as AUC and TSS rows. Verified against ecospat R package reference values on three test species.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All benchmark_result rows include AUC, TSS, and Boyce Index metrics; evaluation pipeline meets the presence-only SDM validation protocol required by the research paper.

target_output.title: GPU-Accelerated Species Distribution Model Benchmarking Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype for computational ecologists benchmarking GPU-accelerated species distribution model algorithms on CHELSA climate scenario projections. Built with Python 3.12, PyTorch 2.3, and CUDA 12.1, running under Slurm on HPC A100 nodes. Results aggregated in DuckDB and tracked via Weights and Biases. Round 3 completes the reproducibility fix (seed_everything with all four library calls) and adds Boyce Index computation to the evaluation pipeline.
target_output.architecture_overview: Script-based research pipeline with no web interface. Slurm job scripts invoke Python training entrypoints that load species occurrence data from Lustre-hosted GBIF CSVs and clip CHELSA climate NetCDF layers via GDAL, then train GPU-accelerated SDM algorithm variants (MaxEntGPU, BRTGPU, BioclimGPU) in PyTorch on A100 nodes. Evaluation metrics (AUC-ROC, TSS, Boyce Index) computed on geographic-blocked held-out test sets. Results written transactionally to DuckDB benchmark result tables on Lustre. Experiment tracking via W&B captures convergence curves, GPU memory utilization, and all hyperparameters including the mandatory seed value.
target_output.technology_stack: Training: Python 3.12, PyTorch 2.3, CUDA 12.1, scikit-learn 1.4, GDAL 3.8. Evaluation: sklearn AUC-ROC, manual Boyce Index (validated against ecospat R reference). Data: DuckDB 0.10, GBIF occurrence CSV, CHELSA SSP2-4.5/SSP5-8.5 NetCDF. Orchestration: Slurm 23.x, conda 23.x environment on Lustre. Experiment tracking: Weights and Biases. CI: GitHub Actions pytest on CPU (CUDA mocked).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Algorithm Training: train MaxEntGPU (presence-background logistic regression on GPU), BRTGPU (gradient boosted regression tree with GPU-accelerated XGBoost backend), BioclimGPU (GPU-parallelized bioclimatic envelope calculations). Data Ingestion: GBIF occurrence CSV loader with geographic blocking for train/test split. Climate Layer Stack: CHELSA NetCDF ingestion, variable selection (BIO1-BIO19), spatial clipping to species range bounding box. Evaluation: AUC-ROC, TSS, and Boyce Index on blocked test set. Result Aggregation: DuckDB queries comparing metric distributions across algorithm variants. Experiment Tracking: W&B run dashboard with convergence curves, metric tables, and full config including seed.
target_output.system_components: Slurm Job Templates (train_sdm.sh for single run, batch_experiment.sh for algorithm sweep). Python Training Entrypoint (src/train.py with seed_everything, OccurrenceLoader, ClimateLayerStack, algorithm dispatch). Algorithm Modules (MaxEntGPU, BRTGPU, BioclimGPU in src/algorithms/). EvaluationEngine (AUC, TSS, Boyce Index; validated outputs). ResultWriter (DuckDB transactional insert). Weights and Biases Logger (hyperparameters, metrics, GPU utilization). DuckDB Benchmark Database (per experiment batch on Lustre). GDAL Raster Processor (CHELSA layer ingestion and reprojection).
target_output.workflows: Training Run Workflow: Researcher submits 'sbatch jobs/train_sdm.sh --species 5219243 --scenario ssp245_2041-2060 --algorithm maxent_gpu --seed 42' to Slurm. The job activates the conda environment, calls seed_everything(42) seeding torch, torch.cuda, numpy.random, and random, loads 3,847 Puma concolor GBIF occurrences with geographic blocking (80/20 train/test), clips 19 CHELSA BIO variables to the species bounding box via GDAL, trains MaxEntGPU for 200 epochs on an A100, and evaluates on the test set. EvaluationEngine computes AUC=0.891, TSS=0.714, and Boyce Index=0.83, writing all three as benchmark_result rows in a single DuckDB transaction. W&B logs the convergence curve, all hyperparameters including seed=42, and GPU memory peak. Comparison Workflow: After a 9-job array completes (3 algorithms x 3 seeds), a researcher opens analysis/compare_runs.ipynb in Jupyter, attaches the DuckDB file, runs SQL grouping benchmark_results by algorithm_variant to produce mean AUC, TSS, and Boyce Index with standard deviation across seeds, and exports the comparison table to CSV for the paper.
target_output.data_model: species_record (taxon_key INT, gbif_occurrence_id BIGINT, decimal_latitude REAL, decimal_longitude REAL, coordinate_uncertainty_m REAL, observed_on DATE, split ENUM train/test). climate_scenario (scenario_id TEXT PK, model TEXT, ssp TEXT, time_period TEXT, variable_list TEXT[]). model_run (run_id UUID PK, taxon_key INT, scenario_id TEXT FK, algorithm_variant TEXT, seed INT [LOGGED TO W&B], wandb_run_id TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP, gpu_peak_memory_gb REAL). benchmark_result (result_id UUID PK, run_id UUID FK, metric_name TEXT CHECK IN (auc_roc, tss, boyce_index), metric_value REAL, dataset_split TEXT, computed_at TIMESTAMP). algorithm_variant (name TEXT PK, config_json TEXT). prediction_grid (grid_id UUID PK, run_id UUID FK, grid_resolution_km REAL, crs TEXT, geotiff_path TEXT).
target_output.api_design: No REST API. Command-line interface via Slurm job scripts invoking Python entrypoints. Key CLI invocations: python src/train.py --species {taxon_key} --scenario {scenario_id} --algorithm {maxent_gpu|brt_gpu|bioclim_gpu} --seed {int} --output_db {duckdb_path}. python src/evaluate.py --run_id {uuid} --db {duckdb_path} outputs benchmark_result rows. python src/compare.py --db {duckdb_path} --group_by algorithm_variant outputs summary statistics TSV. All outputs also logged to W&B run associated with the run_id.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HPC Slurm cluster provides user-level isolation. W&B API key stored only in user shell environment variable (WANDB_API_KEY); never printed to stdout or logged in W&B config. conda environment.yml committed to Git with no secrets. No internet-facing endpoints. All data (GBIF occurrences, CHELSA climate layers) is public scientific datasets; no data governance requirements beyond DuckDB file access permissions on Lustre (user rwx, group r, world none).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Deployment is installation of the conda environment on the HPC cluster: conda env create -f environment.yml creates the sdm_gpu environment in the Lustre shared filesystem home directory. Slurm job scripts in jobs/ reference the conda env by name. New team members install the environment once and submit jobs immediately. GitHub Actions CI runs pytest on a CPU subset (CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES='' forces CPU fallback mode) on every PR to main. GPU integration tests run manually on the HPC before merge when algorithm modules are changed.
target_output.observability: Weights and Biases experiment dashboard tracks: training loss per epoch, AUC and TSS validation metrics per epoch, Boyce Index at run completion, GPU memory utilization (nvidia-smi sampled every 60 seconds via W&B system metrics), seed value in config for reproducibility verification. Slurm job stdout and stderr captured in logs/{job_id}.out and logs/{job_id}.err. DuckDB benchmark_results queryable post-hoc in Jupyter notebook via DuckDB Python API for cross-algorithm comparison.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud costs; runs on lab HPC cluster covered by institutional allocation. W&B free tier (100 GB artifact storage) sufficient for prototype experiments. If artifact storage is exceeded, W&B Artifacts eviction policy set to keep last 10 runs per algorithm variant. Scaling to multi-species experiments: batch_experiment.sh Slurm job array supports up to 100 concurrent tasks on available A100 nodes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (completed rounds 1-2): Slurm job templates, PyTorch GPU training pipeline for MaxEntGPU and BRTGPU, GBIF and CHELSA data loaders, AUC and TSS evaluation, DuckDB result storage, W&B logging. Phase 2 Round 3 (current): Complete seed_everything() reproducibility fix, add Boyce Index to EvaluationEngine, validate against ecospat R reference values, log seed to W&B config. Phase 3: BioclimGPU implementation, multi-species batch experiment support, W&B Sweeps hyperparameter optimization, ensemble prediction aggregation across algorithm variants.
target_output.development_guidelines: seed_everything(seed) must be the first call in train.py main() before any data loading or model initialization -- this is enforced via a pytest fixture that checks function call order. All benchmark_result inserts must use DuckDB transactions (with conn.begin(): ...) to prevent partial result rows from parallel job failures. W&B run names follow convention: {algorithm}_{species_taxon_key}_{scenario_id}_{seed} for traceability. conda environment.yml must pin all package versions; no unpinned dependencies. pytest CPU test suite must pass on every PR; GPU integration tests required only for algorithm module changes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DuckDB file on Lustre can be corrupted by simultaneous writes from parallel Slurm job array tasks -- mitigated by writing separate DuckDB files per job and merging with DuckDB ATTACH after job array completion. Risk: CUDA non-determinism in certain PyTorch operations (e.g., atomicAdd in scatter operations) may prevent byte-perfect reproducibility even with seeding -- mitigated by torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic=True and documenting known non-deterministic operations. Tradeoff: Custom Boyce Index implementation over ecospat R call -- Python implementation chosen to avoid rpy2 dependency on HPC cluster where R may not be installed on GPU nodes.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed DuckDB over SQLite for benchmark results (DuckDB faster for analytical aggregation across 10K+ result rows). Selected W&B over MLflow (team already uses W&B). Geographic blocking train/test split confirmed over random split to avoid spatial autocorrelation bias in AUC estimates. Boyce Index manual Python implementation validated against ecospat R package on 3 test species within 0.02 tolerance.

metadata.notes: Round 3 GPU SDM research prototype; fixes incomplete seed_everything (ISS-001) and missing Boyce Index metric (ISS-002); weak quality as prototype with thin sections appropriate.

profile.domain: Municipal Government - City Climate Action Plan

project_goal.value: Static public information website for a city climate action plan, publishing emissions inventories, policy milestones, and resident action guides, with strong content security to prevent unauthorized modification of official city information.
project_goal.rationale: Replaces a PDF-only distribution model; city council needs an authoritative public-facing policy tracker.

target_users.value: City residents, journalists, and NGO partners reviewing the city's climate commitments and tracking progress toward emissions reduction milestones.
target_users.rationale: Broad public audience with no authentication requirement.

project_class.rationale: No user accounts or dynamic server logic; static site generator minimizes attack surface for official government content.

capabilities.rationale: Static content delivery with privacy-respecting page view analytics.

complexity_level.rationale: Static content site; no server-side services, no user state, no payment flows.

risk_level.rationale: No personal data; primary risk is content defacement undermining city government credibility.

data_sensitivity.rationale: All content is public government information; no PII collected.

external_exposure.rationale: Fully public; open to all city residents and global audiences.

access_model.value: Public read-only. Content updates require GitHub PR with mandatory city IT approval before merge to main.
access_model.rationale: City communications must be reviewed by authorized staff before publication.

feature_scope.value: Emissions inventory pages by sector category, policy milestone tracker with status badges, resident action guides, progress indicators with year-over-year comparison, publication year archive, downloadable PDF policy documents.
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope for public climate policy transparency.

mvp_scope.value: Milestone tracker, sector emissions inventory summaries, three resident action guides, and Cloudflare Analytics integration.
mvp_scope.rationale: Core accountability features needed before city council presentation deadline.

security_baseline.value: Cloudflare Pages security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options) blocking content injection; branch protection on main with required city IT reviewer.
security_baseline.rationale: Protecting official city government content from defacement and clickjacking.

frontend_stack.value: Hugo with Bootstrap 5; Cloudflare Pages for hosting and security header enforcement.
frontend_stack.rationale: Hugo fast build times; Bootstrap 5 for accessible government-style UI; Cloudflare Pages for integrated security headers.

backend_stack.rationale: Static website project class; no backend services required.

data_platform.value: None (content in Git as Markdown and TOML data files; no database).
data_platform.rationale: Git-versioned content workflow for city communications team.

hosting_target.value: Cloudflare Pages CDN with automatic production deploys from main branch.
hosting_target.rationale: Cloudflare Pages provides global CDN, DDoS protection, and integrated header configuration for government site.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Cloudflare Web Analytics (no cookies, no personal data, no fingerprinting) for page view metrics; no user data collected or retained.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: City government cannot collect resident personal data without consent; cookie-free analytics satisfies this constraint.

future_scope.value: Interactive emissions trend chart using Cloudflare D1 for data queries, multilingual support (Spanish), and API endpoint for city open data portal integration.
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap for expanded public engagement.

constraints.value: Hugo build must complete in under 2 minutes; no JavaScript from CDNs outside Cloudflare; must pass WCAG 2.1 AA audit before city council presentation.
constraints.rationale: City IT policy constraints on third-party script usage and accessibility requirements.

observability_baseline.value: Cloudflare Pages build logs; Cloudflare Web Analytics dashboard for page views by sector and milestone page; Cloudflare security event logs for CSP violation reports.
observability_baseline.rationale: City IT monitoring via Cloudflare dashboard only; no additional observability infrastructure.

execution_preference.value: Git-driven content updates with mandatory PR review; GitHub Actions for Hugo build validation before merge.
execution_preference.rationale: City staff content workflow with required IT review gate.

project.goal: Static city climate action plan public information site with content security hardening
project.target_users: City residents, journalists, NGO partners
project.timeline: Live before city council presentation in 4 weeks

security.headers: CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options via Cloudflare Pages _headers
security.branch_protection: main requires city IT reviewer approval
security.csp_reporting: report-uri endpoint for CSP violation collection

data.content: Markdown content files with TOML frontmatter
data.policy_data: Hugo data/ directory TOML files for emissions inventory and milestone data

devops.ci: GitHub Actions (Hugo build, HTML validation, axe accessibility)

constraints.third_party_js: Only Cloudflare-origin scripts allowed by city IT policy

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 3 must resolve the persistent ISS-001 CSP header misconfiguration that has been unresolved through two rounds. The city government's content credibility depends on this being correct. The CSP header needs a specific, concrete specification -- not a placeholder -- with a report-uri endpoint so city IT can monitor any violations. This is a high-severity issue for a government information site.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The round 2 CSP was added but used an overly permissive 'unsafe-inline' value for script-src due to Bootstrap 5 requiring inline event handler support. This round should migrate Bootstrap 5 interactive components to use data-bs attributes only (no inline onclick handlers) so that 'unsafe-inline' can be removed from script-src. Hugo's built-in asset pipeline can bundle Bootstrap JS as a self-hosted asset.
reasoner_reviews.security: The ISS-002 concern about missing branch protection configuration has been deferred two rounds. The GitHub Actions workflow should include a required status check and the main branch protection rule must be documented in the repository README so city IT can verify it is configured.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4-week deadline is manageable. Bootstrap 5 migration away from unsafe-inline requires auditing all templates for inline event handlers -- this is the main implementation risk. Hugo build time for the expected 50-100 page site is well under the 2-minute budget.

frontend.app_structure: Hugo site with layouts/: baseof.html (shared Chrome with Bootstrap 5 navbar and footer), milestone-tracker.html (single layout), emissions-inventory.html (sector template), action-guide.html (single). Content in content/: milestones/, inventory/, guides/. Data in data/: milestones.toml, inventory.toml, progress_indicators.toml.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home (headline milestone progress, sector emissions summary cards, latest action guide). Milestone Tracker (full list of policy commitments with status badges: on_track, delayed, completed; year targets). Emissions Inventory (sector category pages: buildings, transportation, waste, energy; year-over-year comparison tables). Resident Action Guides (three guides: home energy, sustainable transport, food and waste). Publication Archive (TOML-driven list of PDF policy documents with date and description).
frontend.accessibility_notes: Bootstrap 5 components use semantic HTML; all status badges include aria-label (e.g., aria-label='Status: On track'). Milestone progress indicators use role=meter with aria-valuenow attributes. Minimum color contrast ratio 4.5:1 for all text. axe-core CI scan blocks merge on WCAG 2.1 AA violations.
frontend.performance_notes: Hugo builds all pages in under 60 seconds for a 100-page site. Bootstrap CSS self-hosted (bundled via Hugo Pipes PostCSS purge) eliminates external CDN dependency. No JavaScript CDN links -- Bootstrap JS self-hosted from Cloudflare Pages.

security.auth_design: No authentication. Public read-only access to all pages.
security.abuse_prevention: Cloudflare DDoS protection at edge. Branch protection on main requires city IT reviewer approval. No user-submitted content; no forms.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Cloudflare security event logs capture CSP violation reports via report-uri. Cloudflare Web Analytics logs page views without personal data. GitHub Actions audit log shows all PR merges to main.

data.entities: policy_milestone (id TEXT, title TEXT, target_year INT, status ENUM on_track/delayed/completed, sector TEXT, lead_department TEXT, last_updated DATE, description TEXT). emissions_inventory (sector TEXT, year INT, co2e_tonnes INT, percent_change_yoy REAL, data_source TEXT). action_guide (slug TEXT, title TEXT, sector TEXT, steps TEXT[], impact_estimate TEXT). sector_category (slug TEXT, label TEXT, icon_class TEXT). publication_year (year INT, document_list: [{title, pdf_path, published_date}]).
data.storage_design: All data stored as TOML files in Hugo data/ directory and Markdown files in content/ with TOML frontmatter. Hugo range and where functions query data files at build time. No database required.
data.retention_and_deletion: All content versioned in Git history. Cloudflare edge logs retained 7 days (free tier). No personal data collected.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloudflare Pages project connected to GitHub repository. Main branch auto-deploys to production domain (climate.cityname.gov). Feature branches generate preview URLs for content review.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on PR: Hugo build validation, HTML validation via html-validate, axe-core CLI accessibility scan on milestone tracker and emissions inventory pages. All checks required status checks for merge to main. Cloudflare Pages deploys automatically on merge.
devops.observability_stack: Cloudflare Web Analytics (cookie-free, no personal data) for page views by URL. Cloudflare security events for CSP violation report-uri endpoint. GitHub Actions run history for build and deploy audit trail.

ISS-001.detail: Round 1 had no CSP header; round 2 added a CSP but included script-src 'unsafe-inline' to accommodate Bootstrap 5 inline event handlers. This allows inline script injection and provides no meaningful XSS protection on a government information site.
