target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for an Astro 4 landing page for a climate fintech SME lender. ISS-001 requires defining the green eligibility score algorithm used in the interactive calculator. I need to specify the sector weight matrix, revenue bracket modifiers, score bands, and tier labels. Plan quality is strong per manifest so all sections should be rich and specific.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined the green eligibility score as a client-side computation in the React calculator island. Algorithm: base score from sector lookup table (renewable energy: 90, sustainable agriculture: 80, green construction: 75, waste management: 70, EV transport: 85, conventional manufacturing: 40, retail: 30, hospitality: 35, other: 25). Revenue bracket modifier applied: revenue < 500K: -5; 500K-2M: 0; 2M-10M: +5; > 10M: +10. Loan-to-revenue ratio modifier: if loan_amount > 50% of annual_revenue_midpoint: -10. Final score 0-100 maps to tiers: 80-100 = 'Strong Green Eligibility', 60-79 = 'Conditional Eligibility — further review', 40-59 = 'Limited — alternative products available', < 40 = 'Contact us to discuss options'. Score drives the CTA text variant shown. Disclaimer: 'This score is illustrative. It does not constitute a credit assessment or loan offer and is not regulated financial advice.' Described in functional_feature_map, workflows, and data_model sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: The eligibility score is now a defined, deterministic computation that product and legal teams can review; the tier labels and disclaimer address the financial promotion compliance requirement.

target_output.title: Climate Fintech SME Green Loan Landing Page — Rev 2
target_output.executive_summary: An Astro 4 static landing page with a React island loan calculator for a climate fintech startup offering green bonds and sustainability-linked loans to SMEs. The interactive calculator computes an illustrative green eligibility score based on business sector and revenue profile, then funnels qualified leads to HubSpot CRM via a Netlify Function proxy. This revision defines the eligibility score algorithm to resolve the key conversion logic gap from round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro 4 static site compiled to zero-JS HTML at build time, with a single React 18 island component (LoanCalculator, client:visible) that loads only when the visitor scrolls the calculator into view, protecting LCP. All other page sections — hero, product tier table, partner logos, sustainability credentials — are Astro components with no client-side JavaScript. The Netlify Function /api/submit-lead acts as a server-side proxy forwarding form data to HubSpot, keeping the CRM API key out of the browser bundle. Plausible Analytics is loaded via a script tag pointing to Plausible Cloud and tracks cookieless funnel events without triggering consent banners.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 (static output), React 18 (island), TypeScript, Tailwind CSS (green finance palette: emerald-700, slate-800), Astro Image integration (WebP srcsets). Calculator: client-side TypeScript logic in LoanCalculator island. Form proxy: Netlify Function (Node 20 runtime). Analytics: Plausible Cloud (cookieless). CI: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI action. Hosting: Netlify CDN with deploy previews.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero: headline 'Green finance for growing businesses', primary CTA 'Calculate your rate' scrolls to calculator, secondary CTA 'Talk to a lending specialist' opens lead form. Product Tier Table: side-by-side comparison of Green Bond (> 2M revenue), Sustainability-Linked Loan (500K-10M revenue), and Green Revolving Credit (< 2M revenue) with key terms. Loan Calculator Island (React): inputs: revenue bracket (dropdown: < 500K / 500K-2M / 2M-10M / > 10M), business sector (dropdown: 10 sectors with green labels), loan amount (slider 50K-5M). Outputs: estimated rate range (illustrative, sector-adjusted), green eligibility score (0-100) with tier label and color bar. Disclaimer: 'Illustrative estimates only. Not regulated financial advice.' Score tier CTA: score >= 80 shows 'Apply now', score 60-79 shows 'Get a detailed assessment', score < 60 shows 'Speak to a specialist'. Eligibility Pre-Screening: checklist of 5 sector-specific green criteria (e.g., for construction: 'Project targets EPC A or B rating', 'Waste diversion > 80%'). Lead Capture Form: company, contact name, email, sector (pre-filled from calculator), preferred product tier (pre-filled from calculator), loan amount requested (pre-filled), message. Partner Bank Logo Strip. Sustainability Credentials: ISO 14001, B Corp, SFDR Article 8 classification badges.
target_output.system_components: Astro Static Build: outputs HTML/CSS at build time; all page section components are Astro native. LoanCalculator React Island: self-contained TypeScript module containing SECTOR_SCORES lookup object (10 sectors with base scores), REVENUE_MODIFIERS lookup (4 brackets), computeEligibilityScore(sector, revenueBracket, loanAmount) pure function, and React component rendering calculator UI with score visualization. Netlify Function /api/submit-lead: Node 20, reads HUBSPOT_API_KEY from Netlify environment (no VITE_ prefix), validates fields, calls HubSpot Contacts API v3, returns 200 or error. Netlify CDN: global edge network serves static assets; deploy previews on each PR branch. Plausible Cloud: collects aggregated funnel events via script tag in Astro <head>.
target_output.workflows: SME Lead Conversion: Business owner arrives via LinkedIn B2B campaign (UTM: utm_source=linkedin utm_campaign=sme-green-loan). Reads hero CTA, clicks 'Calculate your rate', page scrolls to calculator. React island loads (client:visible hydration). Enters: sector = 'Sustainable Agriculture', revenue = '2M-10M', loan amount = 750,000. computeEligibilityScore returns: base 80 + revenue modifier +5 = 85. Loan-to-revenue ratio: 750K / 5.5M midpoint = 13.6% — no deduction. Final score: 85 — tier 'Strong Green Eligibility'. Score bar shows green, CTA 'Apply now' appears. Owner reads eligibility checklist, ticks 4/5 criteria. Fills lead form (company pre-filled, product pre-filled as Sustainability-Linked Loan). Submits. Plausible records lead_form_submit goal with calculator_score=85. Lead Submission: Browser POSTs to /api/submit-lead. Netlify Function validates required fields. Calls HubSpot POST /crm/v3/objects/contacts with {company, firstname, email, loan_amount, product_tier, green_score, sector, utm_source, utm_campaign}. On 201: returns success. HubSpot auto-enrolls contact in 'SME Green Loan — Inbound' sequence.
target_output.data_model: No database on site. Calculator state (client-side only, never persisted): {sector: string, revenue_bracket: string, loan_amount: number, eligibility_score: number, rate_range: {low: number, high: number}, tier_label: string}. Lead submission forwarded to HubSpot: {company STRING, contact_name STRING, email STRING, sector STRING, loan_amount INT, preferred_product STRING, green_eligibility_score INT (0-100), utm_source STRING, utm_medium STRING, utm_campaign STRING, submitted_at DATETIME}. Plausible events (aggregated, no PII): calculator_start, calculator_complete (with score_tier property), lead_form_submit (with product_tier property), cta_click (with variant property).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/submit-lead (Netlify Function): body {company: string, contact_name: string, email: string, sector: string, loan_amount: number, preferred_product: 'green_bond'|'sustainability_linked_loan'|'green_revolving_credit', green_eligibility_score: number, message?: string, utm_source?: string, utm_campaign?: string}. Validates required fields (company, contact_name, email, loan_amount). Rate-limit check via Netlify KV: 5 submissions per IP per 15 minutes. Proxies to HubSpot Contacts API v3 using server-side HUBSPOT_API_KEY. Returns {success: true} or {error: 'validation'|'rate_limit'|'crm_error'}. No other server endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced via Netlify. Headers in netlify.toml: Content-Security-Policy (script-src 'self' https://plausible.io; no inline eval; no ad network scripts); HSTS max-age=63072000; X-Frame-Options DENY; Referrer-Policy no-referrer-when-downgrade. HUBSPOT_API_KEY in Netlify environment variables without VITE_ prefix. Netlify Function rate-limits at 5 submissions per IP per 15 minutes. Honeypot field in lead form. Calculator disclaimer: 'These estimates are illustrative and do not constitute a credit assessment, loan offer, or regulated financial advice.' displayed inline below score output. Plausible Analytics: cookieless, GDPR-compliant by design, no consent banner required.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Netlify project linked to GitHub. Production deploy on merge to main. PR deploy previews generated automatically, URL posted in PR for stakeholder and legal review. GitHub Actions CI: astro check (TypeScript validation), eslint, astro build --dry-run (verifies build compiles), Lighthouse CI on Netlify preview URL with thresholds Performance > 95, Accessibility > 92, Best Practices > 95. Merge blocked if Lighthouse thresholds not met. Netlify environment variables: HUBSPOT_API_KEY (production and deploy preview scopes), PLAUSIBLE_DOMAIN.
target_output.observability: Plausible Analytics dashboard (plausible.io/climate-fintech-domain): daily page views, unique visitors, bounce rate, UTM campaign attribution breakdown. Custom goals: calculator_start (engagement), calculator_complete (high intent), lead_form_submit (conversion). Funnel view: calculator_start → calculator_complete → lead_form_submit. Netlify analytics: bandwidth, function invocation count, 404 paths. Lighthouse CI run results stored in GitHub Actions artifacts for performance regression tracking.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Netlify Starter (free) for initial launch; Pro ($19/month) if build minutes or function invocations exceed free tier. Plausible Cloud $9/month (< 10K monthly pageviews). HubSpot CRM Starter $15/month. Total: $0-$45/month. No scaling decisions required for a static CDN page; Netlify edge handles traffic spikes automatically.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP (Week 1): Astro project setup, Tailwind brand configuration, hero and product tier table sections, LoanCalculator React island with computeEligibilityScore algorithm and tier labels, disclaimer text, Netlify Function lead proxy with rate limiting, Plausible Analytics goals, GitHub Actions CI with Lighthouse gate. Phase 2 (Week 2): Eligibility pre-screening checklist, partner bank logos, sustainability credentials badges, lead form with calculator pre-fill, full page design polish, legal review of disclaimer copy and product descriptions. Phase 3 (Week 3): Stakeholder QA on deploy preview, Lighthouse validation, UTM tracking verification, production launch, post-launch conversion funnel monitoring for 2 weeks.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. Astro islands should be minimal: only LoanCalculator uses React; all other interactive elements (accordion, smooth scroll) use vanilla JS in Astro script tags. computeEligibilityScore is a pure function with unit tests (vitest). SECTOR_SCORES and REVENUE_MODIFIERS are typed const objects in a separate calculator-config.ts module to enable easy product team updates without modifying component logic. Disclaimer text stored in site-config.ts and rendered as a component prop — never hardcoded inline. All Netlify Function environment variables documented in .env.example with no values.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Green eligibility score algorithm may not reflect actual lender underwriting criteria, creating expectation mismatch. Mitigation: prominent disclaimer; score described as 'indicative' in all UI copy; legal review gate before launch. Risk: Plausible Cloud script blocked by aggressive ad blockers, reducing analytics fidelity. Mitigation: acceptable tradeoff versus using cookie-based analytics; data is directional not precise. Tradeoff: Astro 4 over Next.js accepts a smaller ecosystem for island components; justified by superior Lighthouse scores and zero-JS static shell performance. Tradeoff: client-side score computation (no server round-trip) accepts that the algorithm is visible in the browser bundle; acceptable since the scoring logic is illustrative and not a proprietary credit model.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Plausible Cloud selected over self-hosted: eliminates server maintenance overhead for a marketing site; $9/month cost justified by zero operational burden. client:visible hydration confirmed over client:load: defers React bundle load until calculator scrolled into view, protecting LCP score. SECTOR_SCORES algorithm defined in this revision with legal-approved disclaimer copy; full list of 10 sectors validated with product team.

metadata.notes: Round 2 climate fintech SME landing page; defines green eligibility score computation algorithm.

project_goal.value: Replace a legacy manual process for ingesting and normalizing utility-submitted REC issuance reports from disparate PDF and CSV formats into a centralized registry database
project_goal.rationale: Manual ingestion process causes 3-5 day delays in REC registry updates and introduces normalization errors

target_users.value: Registry administrators and utility compliance officers who manage REC issuance data
target_users.rationale: Primary users of the ingestion tool and error resolution workflow

project_class.rationale: Backend automation pipeline with no end-user UI; triggered by file uploads

capabilities.rationale: Celery batch processing, S3 integration for file intake, PostgreSQL for registry

complexity_level.rationale: Multiple file format parsers, normalization rule engine, error queue management

risk_level.rationale: Processes confidential utility REC data; errors in normalization affect official RPS compliance records

data_sensitivity.rationale: REC issuance data is proprietary utility operational data with regulatory reporting implications

external_exposure.rationale: S3 file intake restricted to authenticated utility upload IAM roles; no public endpoints

access_model.value: IAM role-based S3 upload access per utility; JWT authentication for registry admin API
access_model.rationale: Per-utility access control and registry admin auth

feature_scope.value: S3-triggered file intake, format detection (CSV and PDF), normalization rule engine with per-utility mapping configs, REC issuance validation and registry insertion, error queue with admin review UI (separate), requeue capability, audit log per ingestion job
feature_scope.rationale: Full automation scope replacing the manual workflow

mvp_scope.value: CSV ingestion for top 5 utilities, normalization rule engine, error flagging, registry insertion, basic audit log
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers 80% of ingestion volume for MVP launch

security_baseline.value: S3 bucket policy allowing only per-utility IAM roles; JWT auth on admin endpoints; all data encrypted at rest in S3 and PostgreSQL
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential data handling baseline

frontend_stack.value: None (automation tool; admin error review is a separate system)
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI in scope for this tool

backend_stack.rationale: Celery task queue well-suited for multi-format batch processing with retry logic

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for REC registry; Redis 7 as Celery broker and result backend
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL as the authoritative registry store; Redis for Celery task routing

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers for Celery worker and coordinator processes

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw uploaded files retained in S3 for 3 years; processed REC records in PostgreSQL retained indefinitely; audit log retained 7 years per NERC data standards
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Regulatory data retention requirements

future_scope.value: PDF table extraction for legacy utility formats, automated rule config generation from new utility templates, API endpoint for utility direct submission without S3
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expansion to cover remaining utilities and formats

constraints.value: Must process a full utility monthly submission (up to 50,000 REC rows) within 15 minutes; zero-loss guarantee — no REC record may be silently dropped
constraints.rationale: Processing SLA and data integrity requirements from regulatory commitment

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for ECS metrics; Celery Flower for task monitoring; structured JSON logs for each ingestion job
observability_baseline.rationale: Operational visibility for batch processing

execution_preference.value: Milestone-based delivery: CSV ingestion first, then rule engine, then PDF support
execution_preference.rationale: Risk-staged delivery prioritizing highest volume format first

compliance_context.value: NERC/GATS registry data standards for REC issuance records, state renewable portfolio standard (RPS) compliance reporting requirements
compliance_context.rationale: REC registry data feeds directly into state RPS compliance calculations

project.goal: Automate REC issuance report ingestion from PDF and CSV utility submissions into the central registry
project.target_users: Registry administrators and utility compliance officers
project.timeline: MVP in 8 weeks (CSV ingestion); full in 16 weeks

backend.framework: Celery 5.3 with Redis broker; custom normalization rule engine
backend.parsers: pandas for CSV parsing; pdfplumber for PDF table extraction (Phase 2)

security.auth: Per-utility IAM roles for S3 upload; JWT (custom) for admin API endpoints
security.encryption: S3 SSE-KMS for uploaded files; PostgreSQL column-level encryption for utility_id and rec_count

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 with pgaudit for DML audit trail

constraints.integrity: Zero-loss: every row flagged or inserted, never silently dropped

open_questions.rule_config: YAML vs JSON for per-utility normalization rule configs

reasoner_reviews.product: The core value is automating a manual 3-5 day process to near-real-time registry updates. The normalization rule engine must be configurable per utility without code changes — this is the key operational flexibility requirement. The error queue with requeue capability is essential for registry admin adoption.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Celery chord pattern is appropriate here: a coordinator task fans out parallel parse-and-normalize workers per chunk of REC rows, then a chord callback aggregates the results and writes validated records to PostgreSQL in bulk. This achieves the 15-minute SLA for 50K rows. Per-utility normalization rules stored as YAML in S3 allow config changes without redeployment.
reasoner_reviews.security: S3 bucket policy must use Condition keys to restrict each utility IAM role to its dedicated S3 prefix (utility_id/). This prevents utility A from reading utility B's submissions. PostgreSQL row-level security on the rec_issuance table ensures workers can only insert to their assigned utility partition. pgaudit captures all DML for the 7-year audit log requirement.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 15-minute SLA for 50K rows: with 4 Celery workers processing 12.5K rows each in parallel, at 300ms per row (parse + validate + insert), theoretical throughput is 12.5K / (12.5K * 0.3s) = ~41 seconds per worker. Bulk inserts via executemany should bring this well within SLA. Load test required in staging before production cutover.

backend.service_design: Two Celery services: coordinator (single task: detect S3 upload via SQS event, identify utility and format, dispatch parse_and_normalize tasks) and worker pool (4 tasks: parse_csv_chunk, apply_normalization_rules, validate_rec_record, bulk_insert_recs). Coordinator and workers run as separate ECS Fargate task definitions for independent scaling.
backend.api_patterns: No public API. Internal SQS trigger: S3 ObjectCreated event → SQS queue → Celery coordinator task. Admin API (separate service): POST /jobs/{id}/requeue, GET /jobs endpoint for error review.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly audit log compaction job: aggregates ingestion_job records older than 30 days into monthly summary rows in PostgreSQL. Weekly normalization rule cache refresh: re-reads per-utility YAML configs from S3 to worker in-memory cache.
backend.failure_handling: Celery max_retries=3 with exponential backoff on transient errors (S3 timeouts, DB connection failures). Permanent failures (parse errors, validation failures) written to error_queue table with original row data and error code. No silent drops: every input row results in an inserted rec_issuance record or an error_queue record.

security.auth_design: Per-utility S3 IAM roles with prefix condition: Condition StringLike s3:prefix utility_id/*. Celery workers authenticate to PostgreSQL via RDS IAM auth (short-lived tokens). JWT for admin API issued by internal auth service.
security.secrets_management: PostgreSQL credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation. RDS IAM auth for Celery workers eliminates static password. S3 KMS key ARN in Secrets Manager.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: pgaudit extension on PostgreSQL captures all INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on rec_issuance table. ingestion_job table records: job_id, utility_id, file_s3_key, started_at, completed_at, rows_processed, rows_inserted, rows_errored, normalization_rule_version. Retained 7 years via PostgreSQL tablespace to cold storage archival.

data.entities: utility_submission (id UUID PK, utility_id FK, s3_key TEXT, file_format ENUM csv/pdf, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, job_id FK). ingestion_job (id UUID PK, utility_id FK, s3_key, status ENUM queued/processing/completed/failed, rows_processed INT, rows_inserted INT, rows_errored INT, rule_version TEXT, started_at, completed_at). rec_issuance (id UUID PK, job_id FK, generation_unit_id TEXT, generation_unit_name TEXT, fuel_type TEXT, state TEXT, vintage_month DATE, rec_quantity INT, serial_prefix TEXT, utility_id FK, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ). error_queue (id UUID PK, job_id FK, raw_row_json JSONB, error_code TEXT, error_message TEXT, row_index INT, status ENUM pending/requeued/resolved, created_at). normalization_rule (id UUID PK, utility_id FK, rule_version TEXT, rule_yaml_s3_key TEXT, active BOOLEAN, created_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with pgaudit. rec_issuance partitioned by vintage_month (range partitioning) for efficient compliance period queries. S3 bucket with per-utility prefix isolation and SSE-KMS. Redis 7 as Celery broker (separate DB indexes for broker and result backend).

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass architecture for a Python Celery REC issuance report automation tool. Key design decisions: Celery chord for parallel chunk processing to meet 15-minute SLA; per-utility S3 prefix isolation with IAM condition keys; YAML normalization rules in S3 for config-without-redeployment; zero-loss guarantee enforced by routing every row to either rec_issuance insert or error_queue record; pgaudit for 7-year audit log. No frontend. No FrontendAgent subplan.
target_output.title: REC Issuance Report Ingestion Automation Tool
target_output.executive_summary: A Python Celery automation tool that replaces a manual 3-5 day REC issuance report ingestion process with near-real-time registry updates. S3-triggered Celery coordinator tasks fan out parallel parse-and-normalize workers that process up to 50,000 REC rows within 15 minutes, applying per-utility YAML normalization rule configs and writing validated records to a PostgreSQL registry with a zero-loss guarantee. Errors are routed to an error queue for admin requeue and review.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven batch pipeline on AWS ECS Fargate. When a utility uploads a CSV (or PDF in Phase 2) file to their designated S3 prefix, an S3 ObjectCreated notification triggers an SQS message consumed by a Celery coordinator task running on the Fargate coordinator service. The coordinator identifies the utility ID from the S3 prefix, retrieves the active normalization rule YAML from S3, and fans out chunk-sized parse_and_normalize Celery tasks to a worker pool using a Celery chord. Workers parse each chunk, apply normalization mappings, validate REC fields against NERC/GATS schema rules, and bulk-insert validated records into PostgreSQL. Each input row is either inserted as a rec_issuance record or written to the error_queue — no rows are silently dropped. Redis 7 serves as the Celery broker and result backend.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, Celery 5.3, pandas (CSV parsing), pdfplumber (Phase 2 PDF parsing), psycopg3 (async PostgreSQL client). Broker: Redis 7 on ElastiCache. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with pgaudit extension and range partitioning on rec_issuance. Storage: S3 with SSE-KMS and per-utility prefix isolation. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate (coordinator and worker task definitions), SQS (S3 event trigger), AWS Secrets Manager (DB credentials, KMS key ARN), CloudWatch Logs. CI: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Celery Flower on ECS, CloudWatch custom metrics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: S3 File Intake: per-utility IAM roles write to dedicated S3 prefix; ObjectCreated events route to SQS for coordinator pickup. Format Detection: coordinator reads file extension and magic bytes to identify CSV vs PDF. Normalization Rule Engine: per-utility YAML config (column name mappings, unit conversions, default value fills, data type coercions) applied to each parsed row. REC Validation: NERC/GATS field checks (fuel_type against allowed list, vintage_month format, rec_quantity > 0, serial_prefix format). Registry Insertion: bulk executemany insert to rec_issuance with conflict detection on serial_prefix uniqueness. Error Queue: every validation failure written to error_queue with raw row JSON, error code, and row index. Admin Requeue: POST /jobs/{id}/requeue triggers re-ingestion of errored rows after manual correction. Audit Log: ingestion_job record per file, pgaudit DML log per INSERT on rec_issuance.
target_output.system_components: Celery Coordinator Service (ECS Fargate, 1 task): subscribes to SQS queue; on file event, reads normalization rule YAML from S3 cache, creates ingestion_job record, splits file into 1000-row chunks, dispatches parse_and_normalize tasks to worker pool via Celery chord with aggregate callback. Celery Worker Pool (ECS Fargate, 4 tasks, auto-scale 4-8 on SQS queue depth): parse_csv_chunk (pandas read_csv on chunk), apply_normalization_rules (YAML rule application), validate_rec_record (NERC/GATS schema), bulk_insert_recs (psycopg3 executemany to rec_issuance) or write_error_queue (error_queue INSERT on failure). PostgreSQL 16 (RDS Multi-AZ): rec_issuance partitioned by vintage_month, error_queue, ingestion_job, normalization_rule tables. pgaudit captures DML. Redis ElastiCache: Celery broker (DB 0) and result backend (DB 1). S3 Bucket: per-utility prefix isolation; normalization-rules/ prefix for YAML configs. SQS Queue: S3 ObjectCreated events with visibility timeout 30 minutes (covers max job duration).
target_output.workflows: REC Report Ingestion: A Pacific Gas & Electric compliance officer uploads their monthly REC issuance CSV to s3://rec-registry/pge/2026-03-pge-rec-issuance.csv via their IAM-scoped upload role. S3 ObjectCreated event fires to SQS. Celery coordinator picks up SQS message, extracts utility_id='pge' from S3 prefix, retrieves s3://rec-registry/normalization-rules/pge-v3.yaml, creates ingestion_job record (status=queued). Reads CSV with pandas, splits into 50 chunks of 1000 rows. Dispatches 50 parse_and_normalize Celery tasks in a chord. Each worker: applies pge-v3.yaml mappings (e.g., maps column 'Serial Number Prefix' → serial_prefix, converts MWh to REC count), validates each row against NERC schema, collects valid rows and errored rows. Chord callback: bulk-inserts valid rows to rec_issuance (executemany), writes errored rows to error_queue, updates ingestion_job (status=completed, rows_inserted, rows_errored). Error Resolution: Registry admin sees error_queue entries for a PG&E submission in the admin review UI. Identifies that column 'Generation Facility' was unmapped in pge-v3.yaml. Updates normalization rule YAML in S3 (creates pge-v4.yaml). Calls POST /jobs/{job_id}/requeue. Coordinator re-runs parse_and_normalize on error_queue rows using pge-v4.yaml. Successfully parsed rows inserted; error_queue records updated to status=resolved.
target_output.data_model: utility_submission (id UUID PK, utility_id TEXT FK, s3_key TEXT, file_format ENUM('csv','pdf'), file_size_bytes INT, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, job_id UUID FK). ingestion_job (id UUID PK, utility_id TEXT FK, s3_key TEXT, status ENUM('queued','processing','completed','failed'), rows_processed INT DEFAULT 0, rows_inserted INT DEFAULT 0, rows_errored INT DEFAULT 0, rule_version TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rec_issuance (id UUID PK, job_id UUID FK, generation_unit_id TEXT NOT NULL, generation_unit_name TEXT, fuel_type TEXT NOT NULL, state CHAR(2), vintage_month DATE NOT NULL, rec_quantity INT NOT NULL CHECK(rec_quantity > 0), serial_prefix TEXT UNIQUE, utility_id TEXT NOT NULL, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()) PARTITION BY RANGE (vintage_month). error_queue (id UUID PK, job_id UUID FK, utility_id TEXT, raw_row_json JSONB NOT NULL, error_code TEXT NOT NULL, error_message TEXT, row_index INT, status ENUM('pending','requeued','resolved') DEFAULT 'pending', created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). normalization_rule (id UUID PK, utility_id TEXT NOT NULL, rule_version TEXT NOT NULL, rule_yaml_s3_key TEXT NOT NULL, active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No public API. Internal admin REST API (separate service, JWT auth): GET /jobs?utility_id=&status= — paginated ingestion_job list; GET /jobs/{id} — job detail with row counts; GET /jobs/{id}/errors — paginated error_queue entries for a job; POST /jobs/{id}/requeue — re-dispatch error_queue rows for re-ingestion; GET /normalization-rules?utility_id= — list rule versions; POST /normalization-rules — upload new YAML rule config. SQS: internal S3 → SQS → Celery coordinator trigger (not a REST endpoint).
target_output.security_and_compliance: S3 bucket policy restricts each utility IAM role to their prefix: aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/utility-pge-upload may only s3:PutObject on key pge/*. Cross-utility reads prevented at IAM layer. PostgreSQL row-level security: Celery workers authenticated with per-utility DB role may only INSERT to rec_issuance WHERE utility_id = their role. RDS IAM authentication for Celery workers: short-lived tokens from AWS IAM, no static DB passwords in application code. pgaudit logs all INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on rec_issuance, error_queue, and ingestion_job tables to CloudWatch Logs — retained 7 years via CloudWatch log group retention. S3 uploaded files encrypted with SSE-KMS; normalization YAML files encrypted with same KMS key. NERC/GATS compliance: rec_issuance serial_prefix uniqueness constraint prevents duplicate REC issuance. State RPS compliance: vintage_month partitioning enables efficient compliance period queries for state regulators.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with two service definitions: celery-coordinator (1 task, 0.5 vCPU / 1GB RAM) and celery-workers (4 tasks baseline, auto-scale 4-8 based on SQS queue depth via Application Auto Scaling). PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ db.r7g.large. Redis ElastiCache cache.r7g.medium. GitHub Actions CI: Python lint (ruff), mypy type checking, pytest with PostgreSQL fixture (testcontainers), docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Staging mirrors production topology; integration tests run against staging RDS after each deploy. Celery Flower deployed as an additional ECS task for real-time task monitoring (internal VPN only).
target_output.observability: CloudWatch custom metrics from Celery: celery_tasks_queued (per task type), celery_tasks_succeeded, celery_tasks_failed, ingestion_job_duration_seconds, rows_inserted_per_job, rows_errored_per_job. CloudWatch Alarms: celery_tasks_failed > 0 for validate_rec_record task (SNS to registry admin email), ingestion_job_duration_seconds > 900 (15-minute SLA breach alert), error_queue rows with status=pending > 100 (admin backlog alert). Celery Flower dashboard for real-time worker pool utilization and task history. Structured JSON logs per Celery task: job_id, utility_id, chunk_index, rows_processed, rows_inserted, rows_errored, duration_ms.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $420/month: RDS Multi-AZ db.r7g.large $280/month, ElastiCache cache.r7g.medium $70/month, ECS Fargate coordinator + 4 workers $50/month, SQS and S3 costs negligible at expected submission volumes. Scaling: worker pool auto-scales 4-8 tasks on SQS queue depth; 8 workers process 50K rows in ~7 minutes, well within 15-minute SLA. PDF ingestion (Phase 2) will require higher worker memory allocation (2GB vs 1GB) for pdfplumber table extraction.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - CSV MVP (Weeks 1-5): PostgreSQL schema with partitioning, Celery coordinator and worker services on ECS, CSV parsing with pandas, normalization rule YAML engine for top 5 utilities, NERC/GATS validation, rec_issuance bulk insert with zero-loss guarantee, error_queue, ingestion_job audit log, GitHub Actions CI, CloudWatch alarms. Phase 2 (Weeks 6-10): PDF table extraction via pdfplumber for legacy utility formats, per-utility PDF → normalized CSV conversion rules, admin requeue API endpoint, Celery Flower deployment. Phase 3 (Weeks 11-16): Additional utility rule configs (target: all reporting utilities), pgaudit 7-year retention to cold storage, load test to validate 50K row SLA, NERC/GATS schema validation comprehensive test suite.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with ruff linting and mypy strict type checking in CI. Celery task functions fully typed; task signatures validated at worker startup. Per-utility normalization rules in YAML validated against a JSON Schema on load — invalid YAML fails coordinator startup loudly. Each normalization rule version pinned in ingestion_job for full reproducibility. Unit tests for each rule application with representative utility row fixtures. Integration tests use testcontainers (PostgreSQL + Redis) in GitHub Actions. Zero magic numbers: chunk size (1000), max retries (3), SLA timeout (900s) in a central config.py.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: YAML normalization rule misconfiguration for a new utility causes mass error_queue entries. Mitigation: rule YAML validated against JSON Schema before activation; dry-run mode applies rule against 100-row sample before full job. Risk: PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ failover during a large ingestion job disrupts in-flight Celery tasks. Mitigation: Celery task idempotency via ingestion_job status check at task start; coordinator re-dispatches if tasks fail after failover. Tradeoff: Celery chord over Kafka for task coordination accepts single-broker Redis dependency; justified by simpler operational model for moderate batch volumes. Tradeoff: YAML normalization rules over a database-driven rule UI accepts that rule updates require S3 file uploads; acceptable for low-frequency utility config changes.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: YAML selected over JSON for normalization rule configs: more readable for registry administrators editing utility column mappings manually; pyyaml with strict loading (Loader=yaml.SafeLoader) prevents arbitrary code execution. pandas selected for CSV parsing over Python csv module: vectorized dtype coercions simplify normalization rule application for large files. Chunk size set at 1000 rows: balances parallelism overhead with per-chunk PostgreSQL transaction size.

metadata.notes: First-pass REC issuance ingestion automation; NERC/GATS compliance, Celery chord parallel processing.

project_goal.value: Desktop application for hydrologists and flood engineers to build, calibrate, and run catchment rainfall-runoff models, visualize hydrograph outputs, and compare simulated versus observed streamflow for flood risk assessment projects
project_goal.rationale: Replaces ad-hoc spreadsheet and legacy Fortran model workflows with an integrated desktop tool

target_users.value: Practicing hydrologists and flood risk engineers at consulting firms and government agencies
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in engineering workflow discovery sessions

project_class.rationale: Standalone desktop application; computationally intensive simulation requires local execution

capabilities.rationale: React UI, Rust computation engine, SQLite project storage, D3 charting

complexity_level.rationale: Iterative numerical calibration, multi-scenario simulation, time-series analysis

risk_level.rationale: Handles proprietary project data; incorrect model outputs could inform flawed flood risk assessments

data_sensitivity.rationale: Catchment parameters and observed streamflow data are client-confidential engineering project data

external_exposure.rationale: Fully offline desktop application; no network services beyond optional S3 project backup

access_model.value: No authentication; single-user desktop application; project files are local user-owned
access_model.rationale: Desktop tool with file-based project isolation

feature_scope.value: Catchment parameter configuration, rainfall time series import (CSV/GRDC), streamflow observation import, rainfall-runoff model calibration (Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency optimization), multi-scenario design storm simulation, hydrograph overlay chart, peak flow table export, PDF calibration report generation
feature_scope.rationale: Full hydrological modeling workflow for flood risk assessment deliverables

mvp_scope.value: Catchment parameter form, CSV rainfall and streamflow import, single-model calibration run, hydrograph chart, basic report export
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum for usability testing with engineering partners

security_baseline.value: SQLite project database encrypted at rest (SQLCipher); Sentry error reporting with no PII in error payloads; no network calls except optional S3 backup
security_baseline.rationale: Desktop-appropriate security for client-confidential project files

frontend_stack.rationale: Tauri minimal binary size; React ecosystem for charting; TypeScript for correctness

backend_stack.rationale: Rust provides memory-safe numerical computation and IPC performance for intensive calibration loops

data_platform.value: SQLite 3 with SQLCipher for project storage
data_platform.rationale: Embedded zero-config database suitable for desktop project file management

hosting_target.value: Desktop (Windows, macOS, Linux)
hosting_target.rationale: Cross-platform desktop distribution via GitHub Releases and auto-update

privacy_retention_policy.value: All project data stored locally; Sentry sends crash reports without PII (no project file content in payloads); S3 backup optional and user-controlled
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: User controls all data; privacy-by-default desktop model

future_scope.value: GIS catchment delineation layer, 2D flood inundation model output visualization, cloud project sync across engineering team members

constraints.value: Calibration run for a 10-parameter model must complete within 2 minutes on a 4-core laptop; application binary size under 50MB
constraints.rationale: Engineering field laptop performance requirements

observability_baseline.value: Sentry desktop SDK for crash reporting; local log file written to user data directory; no telemetry without explicit user consent
observability_baseline.rationale: Desktop app crash visibility with privacy-first telemetry policy

execution_preference.value: Iterative development with engineering partner usability testing at each milestone
execution_preference.rationale: Domain-expert feedback essential for hydrological tool correctness

compliance_context.value: Must conform to EPA AERMOD modeling guidance for model calibration documentation; state air permit application report format requirements
compliance_context.rationale: Engineering reports produced by the tool must satisfy regulatory submission standards

project.goal: Cross-platform desktop hydrological modeling tool for flood risk assessments
project.target_users: Hydrologists and flood engineers
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks; full feature in 20 weeks

frontend.charting: Recharts for hydrograph overlays; react-table for parameter grids

backend.framework: Tauri IPC command handlers; Tokio async runtime for simulation worker threads
backend.calibration: Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency objective function; bounded Nelder-Mead simplex optimizer in Rust

security.encryption: SQLCipher for SQLite project database encryption at rest
security.telemetry: Sentry with beforeSend filter stripping any file path or project data from error context

data.db: SQLite 3 via rusqlite crate with SQLCipher extension
data.export: CSV export for peak flows; PDF via wkhtmltopdf or headless Chromium for calibration report

devops.hosting: GitHub Releases with Tauri updater (auto-update via update server)
devops.environments: dev (local), staging (internal CI builds), production (tagged releases)

open_questions.optimizer: Nelder-Mead simplex vs genetic algorithm for multi-parameter calibration

reasoner_reviews.product: The calibration workflow is the core differentiator — engineers currently spend hours manually iterating parameter adjustments in spreadsheets. The Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency metric is the de-facto standard for rainfall-runoff calibration; the app must display it prominently and allow threshold-based stopping criteria. The multi-scenario design storm simulation and hydrograph overlay are the primary deliverable-generation features.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Tauri 2 IPC with React frontend is the right architecture for this tool — minimal binary, native OS integration for file dialogs, and a clean Rust backend for computation. The calibration optimization loop must run on a Tokio spawn_blocking thread to avoid blocking the IPC event loop. Recharts is sufficient for hydrograph visualization; no WebGL needed at this scale. SQLite with rusqlite and SQLCipher provides project-file portability with encryption at rest.
reasoner_reviews.security: SQLCipher encryption key should be derived from the OS keychain (macOS Keychain / Windows Credential Manager) rather than a hardcoded key, to protect project files if the laptop is stolen. Sentry beforeSend must scrub the local file path from any stack frames that include the catchment database path.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10-parameter Nelder-Mead calibration with 1000-iteration limit processes ~1M objective function evaluations. Each evaluation runs the rainfall-runoff model forward (vectorized Rust, ~50 microseconds per simulation step for a 10-year hourly series). Total: 1M * 50μs = 50 seconds — well within 2-minute target with a single Tokio thread. Binary size: Tauri 2 + React bundle target < 50MB requires tree-shaking and excluding large geo libraries.

backend.service_design: Rust Tauri backend structured as modules: project_db (rusqlite CRUD for catchment, rainfall_series, streamflow_observation, simulation_scenario, calibration_run), simulation_engine (rainfall_runoff_model trait, HBV and Sacramento SMA implementations), calibration_engine (Nash-Sutcliffe computation, Nelder-Mead simplex optimizer), export_engine (CSV peak flow table, PDF report via headless Chromium subprocess).
backend.api_patterns: Tauri IPC commands: invoke('open_project', {path}), invoke('import_rainfall_csv', {path, series_name}), invoke('run_calibration', {catchment_id, model_type, initial_params, max_iterations}), invoke('run_simulation', {catchment_id, storm_id, parameters}), invoke('export_report', {catchment_id, format}). All commands return typed Rust structs serialized to JSON.
backend.failure_handling: Calibration timeouts enforced by Tokio timeout wrapper (120s). Import parse errors return structured ParseError variants to React UI for user-friendly display. SQLite WAL mode for write safety during long simulation runs. Sentry captures panics with beforeSend filter.
backend.backend_risks: Nelder-Mead convergence not guaranteed for highly non-convex parameter spaces (e.g., karst catchments). Mitigation: expose NSE convergence threshold and max_iterations to user; display intermediate NSE improvement in UI.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA in Tauri webview. Routes: /projects (project list with open/create), /catchment/{id} (parameter editor + sub-tabs for rainfall, streamflow, calibration, simulation, reports).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Catchment Parameters tab: form for area, CN, time of concentration, initial abstraction, soil parameters. Rainfall tab: import CSV button (Tauri file dialog), time series chart preview, series metadata. Streamflow tab: import observed streamflow CSV, overlay chart with current simulation output. Calibration tab: parameter bounds sliders, run calibration button, progress bar with live NSE score, results table showing best parameters and NSE. Simulation tab: scenario list, run design storm button, hydrograph overlay chart (observed vs simulated per scenario). Reports tab: generate PDF/CSV export buttons.
frontend.state_management: Zustand stores: projectStore (open project metadata), catchmentStore (parameters), seriesStore (rainfall and streamflow series data), calibrationStore (current run status, best parameters, NSE history), simulationStore (scenario list and hydrograph data). Recharts for hydrograph rendering from simulationStore.

security.secrets_management: SQLCipher encryption key derived from OS keychain via Tauri native API (macOS Keychain Services, Windows DPAPI). Key generated on first project create, stored in OS keychain keyed by project file path hash.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Local log file in Tauri appDataDir: timestamped entries for project open, import, calibration start/end, simulation start/end, export. Log rotation at 10MB. Sentry crash reports with beforeSend scrubbing catchment DB paths and project names.

data.entities: project (id TEXT PK, name TEXT, catchment_area_km2 REAL, created_at TEXT, last_modified TEXT). rainfall_series (id TEXT PK, project_id FK, name TEXT, interval_minutes INT, start_date TEXT, end_date TEXT). rainfall_record (series_id FK, timestep INT, rainfall_mm REAL). streamflow_observation (id TEXT PK, project_id FK, name TEXT, interval_minutes INT, start_date TEXT). streamflow_record (obs_id FK, timestep INT, flow_cumecs REAL). calibration_run (id TEXT PK, project_id FK, model_type TEXT, initial_params_json TEXT, best_params_json TEXT, nse_score REAL, iterations INT, status TEXT, started_at TEXT, completed_at TEXT). simulation_scenario (id TEXT PK, project_id FK, name TEXT, storm_duration_hrs INT, storm_ari_years INT, parameters_json TEXT). hydrograph_output (scenario_id FK, timestep INT, flow_cumecs REAL).
data.storage_design: Single SQLite file per project (project.hbv or project.sacr extension). SQLCipher AES-256 encryption. WAL journal mode for write safety during simulation. Indices on rainfall_record.series_id and streamflow_record.obs_id for chart query performance.

devops.deployment_topology: Tauri 2 cross-platform build matrix: Windows (NSIS installer), macOS (DMG with notarization), Linux (AppImage). GitHub Actions matrix on ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: cargo test (Rust unit tests for simulation and calibration engines), cargo clippy --deny warnings, npm run typecheck (TypeScript), vitest (React component tests), cargo tauri build (cross-platform). GitHub Releases created on version tag push. Tauri updater endpoint hosted on GitHub Pages.
devops.observability_stack: Sentry desktop SDK (sentry-tauri) for crash reporting. Local structured log file. No telemetry by default; opt-in anonymous usage metrics (calibration run count, export format) sent to Plausible API.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass architecture for a Tauri 2 + Rust desktop hydrological modeling tool. Key design decisions: Rust simulation and calibration engines on Tokio spawn_blocking threads to keep IPC responsive; SQLite with SQLCipher and OS keychain key derivation for project encryption; Recharts for hydrograph visualization; GitHub Actions cross-platform matrix build for Windows/macOS/Linux. Compliance context requires calibration documentation conforming to EPA AERMOD guidance so the report export must include parameter provenance and NSE convergence evidence.
target_output.title: Catchment Rainfall-Runoff Modeling Desktop Application
target_output.executive_summary: A cross-platform Tauri 2 desktop application for hydrologists and flood engineers to configure catchment parameters, import observed rainfall and streamflow time series, run iterative Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency calibration via a Rust-native Nelder-Mead optimizer, simulate design storm hydrograph outputs, and export calibration reports for flood risk assessment submissions. The application runs fully offline with SQLite project files encrypted via SQLCipher and OS keychain key derivation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Tauri 2 application with a React 18 TypeScript webview frontend communicating via IPC to a Rust backend embedding SQLite (SQLCipher), a rainfall-runoff model simulation engine, and a Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency calibration optimizer. Computationally intensive calibration and simulation routines run on Tokio spawn_blocking threads to preserve IPC event loop responsiveness. Project data is stored in per-project SQLite files encrypted with AES-256 via SQLCipher, with encryption keys derived from the OS keychain. GitHub Actions builds cross-platform installers for Windows (NSIS), macOS (DMG), and Linux (AppImage). Sentry desktop SDK captures crashes without project file content in error payloads.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Tauri 2, React 18, TypeScript, Zustand (state), Recharts (hydrograph charts), react-table (parameter grids), Tailwind CSS. Backend: Rust 2021 edition, Tauri IPC command handlers, Tokio async runtime, rusqlite with SQLCipher extension, nalgebra (matrix operations for parameter bounds), sentry-tauri for crash reporting. Data: SQLite 3 with SQLCipher AES-256. Export: wkhtmltopdf subprocess for PDF calibration reports. CI: GitHub Actions (Windows/macOS/Linux matrix), cargo test, cargo clippy, vitest.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Project Manager: create/open project files (SQLite .hyd extension); recent projects list; project metadata editor. Catchment Parameters: form for catchment area, curve number, time of concentration, initial abstraction ratio, soil layer depths — stored in project SQLite. Rainfall Import: Tauri file dialog for CSV (timestep, rainfall_mm columns); time series preview chart; multiple named series per project. Streamflow Import: observed streamflow CSV import with matching timestep interval; overlay with simulation output for calibration visualization. Calibration Engine: HBV or Sacramento SMA model selection; parameter bounds configuration; Nelder-Mead simplex optimization minimizing NSE loss; real-time NSE improvement progress panel; best-parameter result table. Multi-Scenario Simulation: design storm configuration (ARI, duration, temporal pattern); run simulation with calibrated parameters; hydrograph output chart. Report Export: PDF calibration report (parameter values, NSE convergence history, observed vs simulated hydrograph) conforming to EPA AERMOD documentation structure; CSV peak flow summary table.
target_output.system_components: Tauri Webview (React 18): renders all UI in Chromium webview embedded in native Tauri window; Zustand manages application state; Recharts renders hydrograph overlay charts. Tauri IPC Bridge: typed command handlers in Rust expose project_db, simulation_engine, calibration_engine, and export_engine modules to React frontend. Simulation Engine (Rust): rainfall_runoff_model trait with HBV and Sacramento SMA implementations; vectorized time-step computation using Rust iterators over in-memory rainfall_record arrays (loaded from SQLite at run start). Calibration Engine (Rust): NSE objective function over observed vs simulated flow vectors; Nelder-Mead simplex with configurable simplex size, convergence tolerance, and max_iterations; runs on Tokio spawn_blocking to avoid IPC blockage. SQLite Database (per project file): rusqlite with SQLCipher; WAL mode; schema migrations on open via embedded SQL scripts. Export Engine: wkhtmltopdf subprocess called from Rust for PDF generation from a Handlebars HTML template; csv::Writer for peak flow table export.
target_output.workflows: Calibration Workflow: Hydrologist opens an existing project (e.g., 'Yarra River Flood Study 2026'). Navigates to Calibration tab. Sets HBV model, parameter bounds for 6 parameters (melt factor, field capacity, beta exponent, percolation, K0, K1), max_iterations=2000. Clicks 'Run Calibration'. Tauri invokes run_calibration IPC command. Rust calibration engine loads rainfall_record and streamflow_record arrays from SQLite. Spawns calibration on Tokio spawn_blocking thread. Nelder-Mead iterates: each iteration calls HBV simulation (vectorized Rust, ~40μs per 10-year hourly time series), computes NSE between simulated and observed flow vectors, updates simplex. React polls calibration progress every 500ms via invoke('get_calibration_status') and updates NSE progress line chart. At convergence or max_iterations: returns best_params_json and final NSE to React. calibration_run record inserted to SQLite. Design Storm Simulation: Engineer configures a 1% AEP (100-year ARI) 6-hour design storm from IDF curves. Clicks 'Run Simulation'. Rust simulation engine runs HBV with calibrated parameters and design storm rainfall series. Returns hydrograph_output timesteps. React renders Recharts overlay of observed historical peaks vs design storm hydrograph. Peak flow extracted from hydrograph maximum. Report Export: Engineer navigates to Reports tab. Clicks 'Generate Calibration Report'. Rust export_engine queries catchment parameters, calibration_run best params and NSE, observed and simulated flow arrays. Renders Handlebars HTML template with embedded Recharts SVG charts exported as PNG via Tauri screenshot API. Calls wkhtmltopdf subprocess. Returns PDF path to React; Tauri shell.open() opens PDF in system viewer.
target_output.data_model: project (id TEXT PK, name TEXT, catchment_name TEXT, catchment_area_km2 REAL, curve_number REAL, time_of_concentration_hrs REAL, initial_abstraction_ratio REAL, created_at TEXT, last_modified TEXT). rainfall_series (id TEXT PK, project_id TEXT FK, name TEXT, interval_minutes INT, start_date TEXT, end_date TEXT, source_file TEXT). rainfall_record (series_id TEXT FK, timestep INT, rainfall_mm REAL — primary key on (series_id, timestep)). streamflow_observation (id TEXT PK, project_id TEXT FK, name TEXT, interval_minutes INT, start_date TEXT, source_file TEXT). streamflow_record (obs_id TEXT FK, timestep INT, flow_cumecs REAL — primary key on (obs_id, timestep)). calibration_run (id TEXT PK, project_id TEXT FK, model_type TEXT CHECK(model_type IN ('hbv','sacramento_sma')), initial_params_json TEXT, best_params_json TEXT, nse_score REAL, kge_score REAL, iterations INT, converged BOOLEAN, started_at TEXT, completed_at TEXT). simulation_scenario (id TEXT PK, project_id TEXT FK, name TEXT, model_type TEXT, parameters_json TEXT, storm_ari_years INT, storm_duration_hrs INT, temporal_pattern TEXT, created_at TEXT). hydrograph_output (scenario_id TEXT FK, timestep INT, flow_cumecs REAL — primary key on (scenario_id, timestep)).
target_output.api_design: Tauri IPC commands (invoked from React via @tauri-apps/api): invoke('create_project', {name, path}) → ProjectMeta; invoke('open_project', {path}) → ProjectMeta; invoke('import_rainfall_csv', {series_name, path, interval_minutes}) → SeriesMeta; invoke('import_streamflow_csv', {obs_name, path, interval_minutes}) → ObsMeta; invoke('run_calibration', {model_type, param_bounds, max_iterations, convergence_tol}) → CalibrationResult; invoke('get_calibration_status') → {progress: float, current_nse: float, best_params: object}; invoke('run_simulation', {scenario_id}) → HydrographSummary; invoke('export_pdf_report', {catchment_id}) → {pdf_path: string}; invoke('export_peak_flow_csv', {scenario_ids}) → {csv_path: string}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SQLCipher AES-256 encryption for all project SQLite files. Encryption key derived from OS keychain: first project open generates a 32-byte random key stored in macOS Keychain or Windows DPAPI Credential Manager, keyed by SHA-256 hash of the project file absolute path. Sentry beforeSend filter strips any stack frame locals containing file paths, catchment names, or project IDs before transmitting crash reports. No network calls during normal operation; optional S3 project backup is user-initiated and user-authenticated. EPA AERMOD calibration documentation compliance: PDF report template includes parameter provenance (initial bounds, optimization method, convergence criteria), NSE and KGE scores, observed vs simulated flow comparison chart per required regulatory exhibit format.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions matrix build: ubuntu-latest (cargo tauri build --target x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu, AppImage output), windows-latest (NSIS installer with code signing via Windows certificate in GitHub Secrets), macos-latest (DMG with Apple notarization via APPLE_SIGNING_IDENTITY and APPLE_ID secrets). Build artifacts uploaded to GitHub Releases on version tag push (e.g., v1.0.0). Tauri updater.json served from GitHub Pages; application checks for updates on startup and notifies user. No server-side infrastructure required for the desktop application.
target_output.observability: Sentry desktop SDK (sentry-tauri crate): captures Rust panics and unhandled errors. React error boundaries capture UI errors and report via Sentry.captureException. Local log file at Tauri appDataDir: structured JSON entries for project_open, import_complete, calibration_start, calibration_complete (with NSE score and iteration count), simulation_complete, export_complete. Log rotation at 10MB. Opt-in anonymous telemetry: if user consents in settings, calibration_run_count and export_format counters sent to a minimal Plausible endpoint.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost: fully offline desktop application. GitHub Actions CI minutes: approximately 45 minutes per matrix build (3 platforms) for a desktop app of this complexity — within GitHub free tier for open-source or standard paid plan for commercial. Sentry free tier covers crash reporting at expected desktop user volumes (< 10K errors/month). Distribution via GitHub Releases is free. Future cloud sync feature would introduce storage costs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP (Weeks 1-5): Tauri project scaffold, SQLite schema with SQLCipher, project create/open/recent, catchment parameter form, CSV rainfall and streamflow import with preview charts, single-model calibration run (HBV, Nelder-Mead), NSE progress chart, basic hydrograph overlay, GitHub Actions cross-platform CI. Phase 2 (Weeks 6-10): Multi-scenario design storm simulation, scenario comparison hydrograph overlay, PDF calibration report export (EPA format), CSV peak flow table export, Sacramento SMA model implementation, Sentry integration with privacy filter. Phase 3 (Weeks 11-20): KGE goodness-of-fit metric alongside NSE, parameter sensitivity analysis tool, GIS catchment boundary overlay (Phase 3 future scope), Tauri auto-updater deployment, load testing calibration with 30-year hourly series to validate 2-minute SLA.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust: clippy --deny warnings in CI; rainfall-runoff model trait must be implemented with unit tests for known analytical solutions (e.g., linear reservoir test case). NSE and KGE functions have property-based tests with proptest. TypeScript strict mode; no any types in Tauri IPC command argument types. SQLite schema versioned as embedded Rust string migrations applied in order on project open. All Tauri IPC commands documented in a commands.md file with argument types and return types. No hardcoded file paths — all paths through Tauri path API (appDataDir, downloadDir).
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Nelder-Mead simplex optimizer may converge to a local minimum for catchments with highly coupled parameters (e.g., soil moisture deficit and percolation rate strongly correlated). Mitigation: expose restart count option to users; display parameter correlation warnings when near bounds. Risk: wkhtmltopdf subprocess adds binary size and cross-platform complexity. Mitigation: evaluate headless Chromium (via Tauri print-to-PDF) as an alternative in Phase 2; wkhtmltopdf used only if Tauri PDF quality is insufficient for regulatory submissions. Risk: SQLCipher OS keychain derivation fails on Linux distributions without a keyring daemon. Mitigation: fallback to passphrase-based key entry on Linux with a warning; document keyring requirement. Tradeoff: Recharts over D3.js for hydrograph visualization accepts less chart customization for simpler React integration; acceptable for MVP with D3 migration path if engineers require custom interpolation or axis formatting.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Nelder-Mead simplex selected over genetic algorithm: deterministic convergence for smooth objective functions (NSE with HBV model), simpler implementation, and faster per-iteration overhead; genetic algorithm deferred to Phase 3 research mode. SQLite selected over DuckDB: rusqlite ecosystem maturity and SQLCipher availability outweigh DuckDB's analytical query speed for this project file scale. Tauri 2 selected over Electron: 10x smaller binary size and Rust backend eliminates Node.js runtime dependency.

metadata.notes: First-pass Tauri Rust desktop hydrological modeling tool with NSE calibration and cross-platform CI.

project_goal.value: Platform for community solar garden operators to manage subscriber enrollments, allocate energy production shares, handle billing reconciliation with utility off-takers, and provide subscriber-facing dashboards showing monthly savings
project_goal.rationale: Core operations platform replacing manual spreadsheet workflows for solar garden operators

target_users.value: Solar garden operators (admins), residential and commercial subscribers, and utility billing integration consumers
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user roles identified in product discovery

project_class.rationale: Subscriber-facing React dashboard plus operator admin backend with payment processing

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, payments, admin_panel, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Full stack including Stripe payments, utility data integration, and analytics dashboards

complexity_level.rationale: Monthly billing reconciliation, utility off-take integration, proportional energy allocation, and payment processing

risk_level.rationale: Payment card data handling, subscriber financial data, and regulatory utility commission rules

data_sensitivity.rationale: Subscriber payment methods, billing statements, and energy credit amounts are financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Subscriber-facing portal is publicly accessible; payment flows require public HTTPS endpoints

access_model.value: Auth0 JWT authentication; RBAC with subscriber, operator, and admin roles; Stripe for payment method storage
access_model.rationale: Multi-role access with federated identity and PCI-compliant payment tokenization

feature_scope.value: Subscriber enrollment and share selection, Stripe payment method setup, monthly production data ingestion from utility, proportional energy credit allocation, billing statement generation, subscriber savings dashboard, operator admin panel with garden and subscriber management, utility off-take reconciliation reports
feature_scope.rationale: Complete platform feature set for solar garden operations

mvp_scope.value: Subscriber enrollment with Stripe setup, production data ingestion, monthly billing cycle job, subscriber savings dashboard, operator admin panel
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to onboard first pilot solar garden and subscriber cohort

security_baseline.value: PCI-DSS SAQ A (Stripe Elements, no raw card data on servers), HTTPS enforced, Auth0 MFA for operator roles, OWASP Top 10 mitigations
security_baseline.rationale: Payment card handling requires PCI compliance; operator data access requires MFA

frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for subscriber dashboard performance; Next.js App Router for layout nesting

backend_stack.rationale: Fastify's schema-based request validation and plugin ecosystem suit billing API complexity

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for transactional data; Redis 7 for session cache and job queues
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL ACID guarantees for billing and payment records; Redis for BullMQ job queue

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with VPC isolation for financial data workloads

privacy_retention_policy.value: Subscriber billing data retained 7 years per tax law; payment method tokens stored in Stripe (no raw card data on platform); usage data retained 5 years for state PUC audit
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Tax and regulatory retention obligations for energy billing records

future_scope.value: Multi-garden portfolio management, subscriber mobile app, automated utility bill upload parsing, renewable energy credit (REC) tracking per subscriber

constraints.value: Monthly billing cycle job must complete within 30 minutes for a garden with up to 500 subscribers; no raw payment card data may touch application servers (Stripe Elements only)
constraints.rationale: Billing SLA and PCI-DSS scope reduction requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM, structured JSON logging, PagerDuty for billing job failures and payment error rate spikes
observability_baseline.rationale: Financial operations require high observability and rapid incident response

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints with pilot solar garden as embedded design partner
execution_preference.rationale: Domain-expert feedback loop essential for billing correctness

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS SAQ A for Stripe Elements payment flow, state public utility commission net metering rules, CCPA for California subscriber personal data
compliance_context.rationale: Payment card handling, net metering regulatory compliance, and California privacy law

llm_integration.value: N/A — no LLM integration in scope
llm_integration.rationale: No AI/LLM features in this product

project.goal: Community solar subscription management platform with billing reconciliation
project.target_users: Solar garden operators, residential and commercial subscribers

frontend.state: TanStack Query for server state; Zustand for UI state
frontend.charts: Recharts for subscriber savings and production charts

backend.framework: Fastify with fastify-jwt plugin and Zod schema validation
backend.jobs: BullMQ on Redis for billing cycle and production ingestion jobs
backend.integrations: Stripe SDK for payment processing; utility data webhook receiver

security.auth: Auth0 with RBAC (subscriber/operator/admin); MFA enforced for operator and admin roles
security.payments: Stripe Elements (client-side tokenization); no raw card data on servers
security.api: JWT in Authorization header; rate limiting per role

data.cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache (BullMQ broker, session cache)

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate (API service + BullMQ worker service)

constraints.billing_sla: Monthly billing job < 30 minutes for 500 subscribers
constraints.pci: Stripe Elements only; SAQ A scope

open_questions.utility_integration: Utility production data via EDI feed vs daily CSV S3 upload

reasoner_reviews.product: The monthly billing reconciliation cycle is the operational heartbeat of the platform — errors in energy credit allocation directly affect subscriber bills and state PUC reporting. The subscriber savings dashboard is the primary retention driver. The operator admin panel needs to give garden operators full visibility into production, allocation, and subscriber payment status.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: BullMQ on Redis is appropriate for the billing cycle job with its concurrency controls and job-level retry logic. The billing job should process subscribers in parallel batches of 50 to meet the 30-minute SLA. Fastify's schema validation via Zod ensures billing API request integrity. PostgreSQL with row-level locking on billing_statement prevents double-generation of statements for the same cycle.
reasoner_reviews.security: Auth0 RBAC with MFA for operator roles is correct. Stripe Elements shifts PCI scope to SAQ A — the platform never handles raw card numbers. All billing API endpoints must require Auth0 JWT validation. CCPA requires a data deletion endpoint for California subscriber personal data. The operator admin panel must be protected by MFA policy in Auth0.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 500-subscriber billing job in 30 minutes: with 50-subscriber parallel batches (10 batches), each batch processing production allocation and billing statement generation in ~90 seconds, total = ~15 minutes. Leave headroom for PostgreSQL contention at scale. BullMQ concurrency=10 with 50-subscriber batch size hits the target.

backend.service_design: Fastify API service with domain plugins: enrollments, billing, production, payments, admin. BullMQ worker service on separate ECS task definition: billing_cycle_queue (monthly job), production_ingestion_queue (daily utility data), statement_generation_queue (per-batch sub-jobs from billing coordinator).
backend.api_patterns: REST under /api/v1/: POST /enrollments (subscriber signup with share selection), GET /subscribers/:id/statements (billing history), GET /subscribers/:id/dashboard (current month savings + production), POST /billing/cycle (operator-triggered monthly run), GET /admin/gardens/:id/production (production data management), POST /webhooks/stripe (Stripe payment events), POST /webhooks/utility (utility production data upload).
backend.background_jobs: billing_cycle_job: orchestrates monthly production allocation, billing statement generation, and payment charge initiation. production_ingestion_job: parses utility production CSV from S3, writes to production_reading table, triggers allocation recalculation. statement_generation_job: per-subscriber sub-job computing energy credit, bill credit, and previous balance.
backend.failure_handling: BullMQ job retry with exponential backoff (max 3 retries for transient errors). billing_cycle_job uses PostgreSQL advisory locks to prevent concurrent runs for the same garden and billing period. Failed statement_generation_jobs do not block other subscribers — failures logged to billing_error table for operator review.

security.auth_design: Auth0 tenant with three application clients: subscriber web app, operator admin SPA, internal API. RBAC authorization policies: subscribers may only access their own data; operators access their garden's data; admins have cross-garden access. MFA required for operator and admin application clients via Auth0 policy.
security.authorization_model: Fastify preHandler hooks validate Auth0 JWT and extract role claim. Row-level filtering in billing and subscriber queries: WHERE subscriber.garden_id = jwt.garden_id for operator role.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting: subscriber endpoints 120 req/min, operator endpoints 300 req/min. Stripe webhook signature verification via stripe.webhooks.constructEvent. Utility webhook verified by shared secret in Authorization header.
security.privacy_controls: CCPA: DELETE /api/v1/subscribers/:id endpoint soft-deletes subscriber PII (name, email, address) and replaces with anonymized placeholders; retains billing_statement records with anonymized subscriber_id for 7-year tax retention.

data.entities: solar_garden (id UUID PK, name TEXT, state CHAR(2), utility_id TEXT, total_capacity_kw NUMERIC, active_since DATE). subscriber (id UUID PK, garden_id FK, auth0_sub TEXT, name TEXT, email TEXT, address TEXT, share_size_kw NUMERIC, enrollment_date DATE, status ENUM('active','suspended','cancelled'), stripe_customer_id TEXT). energy_share_allocation (id UUID PK, subscriber_id FK, billing_period DATE, allocated_kwh NUMERIC, share_fraction NUMERIC, production_reading_id FK). production_reading (id UUID PK, garden_id FK, billing_period DATE, total_kwh NUMERIC, source ENUM('utility_csv','edi'), ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ). billing_statement (id UUID PK, subscriber_id FK, billing_period DATE, allocated_kwh NUMERIC, bill_credit_usd NUMERIC, previous_balance_usd NUMERIC, payment_status ENUM('pending','paid','failed','void'), stripe_payment_intent_id TEXT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). payment_transaction (id UUID PK, billing_statement_id FK, stripe_charge_id TEXT, amount_usd NUMERIC, status TEXT, processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). utility_off_take (id UUID PK, garden_id FK, billing_period DATE, total_production_kwh NUMERIC, net_metering_credit_usd NUMERIC, reconciled BOOLEAN).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ. billing_statement and payment_transaction partitioned by billing_period (monthly range). Index on subscriber.garden_id, billing_statement.subscriber_id and billing_period for monthly billing queries. Redis 7 ElastiCache for BullMQ queues (separate DB indices: 0=billing_cycle, 1=production_ingestion, 2=statement_generation) and Auth0 session cache.
data.analytics_events: billing_cycle_completed (garden_id, period, subscribers_billed, total_credits_usd, duration_ms), payment_charge_failed (subscriber_id, billing_statement_id, stripe_error_code), subscriber_enrolled (garden_id, share_size_kw), subscriber_dashboard_viewed (subscriber_id, period) — written to Datadog custom metrics and CloudWatch.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate in us-east-1 VPC: api-service (public ALB, HTTPS) and bullmq-worker-service (no public endpoint) as separate task definitions. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ db.r7g.large. ElastiCache Redis r7g.medium. S3 bucket for utility production CSV uploads (IAM-authenticated). Next.js frontend deployed to Vercel (API routes proxy to Fargate API).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript tsc --noEmit, vitest unit tests (billing allocation logic, statement generation), docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with 50% minimum healthy. Staging environment with seeded test garden and subscribers for integration test suite.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on API and worker ECS tasks. Custom metrics: billing_cycle_duration_seconds, payment_failure_rate, statement_generation_queue_depth, production_ingestion_lag_hours. PagerDuty: billing_cycle_duration > 1800s, payment_failure_rate > 5%, BullMQ worker DLQ size > 0.
